**Western literature**, also known as **European literature**,[[1]](#cite_note-EB-1) is the [literature](/wiki/Literature "Literature") written in the context of [Western culture](/wiki/Western_culture "Western culture") in the [languages of Europe](/wiki/Languages_of_Europe "Languages of Europe"), and is shaped by the periods in which they were conceived, with each period containing prominent western authors, poets, and pieces of literature.

The best of Western literature is considered to be the [Western canon](/wiki/Western_canon "Western canon"). The list of works in the Western canon varies according to the critic's opinions on [Western culture](/wiki/Western_culture "Western culture") and the relative importance of its defining characteristics. Different literary periods held great influence on the literature of Western and European countries, with movements and political changes impacting the prose and poetry of the period. The 16th Century is known for the creation of Renaissance literature,[[2]](#cite_note-:13-2) while the 17th century was influenced by both Baroque and Jacobean forms.[[3]](#cite_note-:1-3) The 18th century progressed into a period known as the Enlightenment Era for many western countries.[[4]](#cite_note-:19-4) This period of military and political advancement influenced the style of literature created by French, Russian and Spanish literary figures.[[4]](#cite_note-:19-4) The 19th century was known as the Romantic era, in which the style of writing was influenced by the political issues of the century, and differed from the previous classicist form.[[5]](#cite_note-:10-5)

## Early medieval Latin literature

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Western_literature&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early medieval Latin literature")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e1/Boethius_initial_consolation_philosophy.jpg/250px-Boethius_initial_consolation_philosophy.jpg)](/wiki/File:Boethius_initial_consolation_philosophy.jpg)

A depiction of [Boethius](/wiki/Anicius_Manlius_Severinus_Boethius "Anicius Manlius Severinus Boethius") teaching his students (1385). Boethius, a 6th-century Christian philosopher, helped keep alive the classic tradition in post-[Roman Italy](/wiki/Roman_Italy "Roman Italy").

As the [Western Roman Empire](/wiki/Western_Roman_Empire "Western Roman Empire") declined, the Latin tradition was kept alive by writers such as [Cassiodorus](/wiki/Cassiodorus "Cassiodorus"), [Boethius](/wiki/Anicius_Manlius_Severinus_Boethius "Anicius Manlius Severinus Boethius"), and [Symmachus](/wiki/Quintus_Aurelius_Symmachus "Quintus Aurelius Symmachus"). The liberal arts flourished at [Ravenna](/wiki/Ravenna "Ravenna") under [Theodoric](/wiki/Theodoric_the_Great "Theodoric the Great"), and the Gothic kings surrounded themselves with masters of [rhetoric](/wiki/Rhetoric "Rhetoric") and of [grammar](/wiki/Grammar "Grammar"). Some lay schools remained in Italy, and noted scholars included [Magnus Felix Ennodius](/wiki/Magnus_Felix_Ennodius "Magnus Felix Ennodius"), [Arator](/wiki/Arator "Arator"), [Venantius Fortunatus](/wiki/Venantius_Fortunatus "Venantius Fortunatus"), [Felix the Grammarian](/w/index.php?title=Felix_the_Grammarian&action=edit&redlink=1 "Felix the Grammarian (page does not exist)"), [Peter of Pisa](/wiki/Peter_of_Pisa "Peter of Pisa"), [Paulinus of Aquileia](/wiki/Paulinus_of_Aquileia "Paulinus of Aquileia"), and many others.

The later establishment of the medieval universities of [Bologna](/wiki/Bologna "Bologna"), [Padua](/wiki/Padua "Padua"), [Vicenza](/wiki/Vicenza "Vicenza"), [Naples](/wiki/Naples "Naples"), [Salerno](/wiki/Salerno "Salerno"), [Modena](/wiki/Modena "Modena") and [Parma](/wiki/Parma "Parma") helped to spread culture and prepared the ground in which the new [vernacular literature](/wiki/Vernacular_literature "Vernacular literature") developed.[[6]](#cite_note-duecento-e-trecento-6) Classical traditions did not disappear, and affection for the memory of Rome, a preoccupation with politics, and a preference for practice over theory combined to influence the development of Italian literature.[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBartoliOelsner1911898-7)

## High medieval literature

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Western_literature&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: High medieval literature")]

### Trovatori

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Western_literature&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Trovatori")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/8/85/Rambertino_Buvalelli.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rambertino_Buvalelli.jpg)

The beginning of [Rambertino Buvalelli](/wiki/Rambertino_Buvalelli "Rambertino Buvalelli")'s *D'un saluz uoill m'entremetre*, with a decorated initial D, and Rambertino's name (*Lanbertin de Buualel*) at top

The earliest vernacular literary tradition in Italy was in [Occitan](/wiki/Occitan_language "Occitan language"), spoken in parts of northwest Italy. A tradition of vernacular [lyric poetry](/wiki/Lyric_poetry "Lyric poetry") arose in [Poitou](/wiki/Poitou "Poitou") in the early 12th century and spread south and east, eventually reaching Italy by the end of the 12th century. The first [troubadours](/wiki/Troubadours "Troubadours") (*trovatori* in Italian), as these Occitan lyric poets were called, to practise in Italy were from elsewhere, but the high aristocracy of the northern Italy was ready to patronise them.[[8]](#cite_note-8) It was not long before native Italians adopted Occitan as a vehicle for poetic expression.

Among the early patrons of foreign troubadours were especially the [House of Este](/wiki/House_of_Este "House of Este"), the [Da Romano](/wiki/Da_Romano "Da Romano"), [House of Savoy](/wiki/House_of_Savoy "House of Savoy"), and the [Malaspina](/wiki/Malaspina_family "Malaspina family"). [Azzo VI of Este](/wiki/Azzo_VI_of_Este "Azzo VI of Este") entertained the troubadours [Aimeric de Belenoi](/wiki/Aimeric_de_Belenoi "Aimeric de Belenoi"), [Aimeric de Peguilhan](/wiki/Aimeric_de_Peguilhan "Aimeric de Peguilhan"), [Albertet de Sestaro](/wiki/Albertet_de_Sestaro "Albertet de Sestaro"), and [Peire Raimon de Tolosa](/wiki/Peire_Raimon_de_Tolosa "Peire Raimon de Tolosa") from [Occitania](/wiki/Occitania "Occitania") and [Rambertino Buvalelli](/wiki/Rambertino_Buvalelli "Rambertino Buvalelli") from [Bologna](/wiki/Bologna "Bologna"), one of the earliest Italian troubadours. Azzo VI's daughter, [Beatrice](/wiki/Beatrice_d%27Este_(died_1226) "Beatrice d'Este (died 1226)"), was an object of the early poets "[courtly love](/wiki/Courtly_love "Courtly love")". Azzo's son, [Azzo VII](/wiki/Azzo_VII_of_Este "Azzo VII of Este"), hosted [Elias Cairel](/wiki/Elias_Cairel "Elias Cairel") and [Arnaut Catalan](/wiki/Arnaut_Catalan "Arnaut Catalan"). Rambertino was named *[podestà](/wiki/Podest%C3%A0 "Podestà")* of [Genoa](/wiki/Genoa "Genoa") in 1218 and it was probably during his three-year tenure there that he introduced Occitan lyric poetry to the city, which later developed a flourishing Occitan literary culture.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a4/BnF_ms._12473_fol._109_-_Sordel_de_Mantoue_%281%29.jpg/250px-BnF_ms._12473_fol._109_-_Sordel_de_Mantoue_%281%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:BnF_ms._12473_fol._109_-_Sordel_de_Mantoue_(1).jpg)

[Sordello](/wiki/Sordello "Sordello") from a 13th-century manuscript

The [margraves of Montferrat](/wiki/Margraves_of_Montferrat "Margraves of Montferrat")—[Boniface I](/wiki/Boniface_I_of_Montferrat "Boniface I of Montferrat"), [William VI](/wiki/William_VI_of_Montferrat "William VI of Montferrat"), and [Boniface II](/wiki/Boniface_II_of_Montferrat "Boniface II of Montferrat")—were patrons of Occitan poetry. Among the Genoese troubadours were [Lanfranc Cigala](/wiki/Lanfranc_Cigala "Lanfranc Cigala"), [Calega Panzan](/wiki/Calega_Panzan "Calega Panzan"), [Jacme Grils](/wiki/Jacme_Grils "Jacme Grils"), and [Bonifaci Calvo](/wiki/Bonifaci_Calvo "Bonifaci Calvo"). Genoa was also the place of the genesis of the *podestà*-troubadour phenomenon: men who served in several cities as *podestàs* on behalf of either the [Guelph or Ghibelline](/wiki/Guelphs_and_Ghibellines "Guelphs and Ghibellines") party and who wrote [political poetry](/wiki/Political_poetry "Political poetry") in Occitan. Rambertino Buvalelli was the first *podestà*-troubadour and in Genoa there were the Guelphs [Luca Grimaldi](/wiki/Luca_Grimaldi "Luca Grimaldi") and [Luchetto Gattilusio](/wiki/Luchetto_Gattilusio "Luchetto Gattilusio") and the Ghibellines [Perceval](/wiki/Perceval_Doria "Perceval Doria") and [Simon Doria](/wiki/Simon_Doria "Simon Doria").

Perhaps the most important aspect of the Italian troubadour phenomenon was the production of [chansonniers](/wiki/Chansonnier "Chansonnier") and the composition of *[vidas](/wiki/Vida_(Occitan_literary_form) "Vida (Occitan literary form)")* and *[razos](/wiki/Razo "Razo")*.[[10]](#cite_note-10) [Uc de Saint Circ](/wiki/Uc_de_Saint_Circ "Uc de Saint Circ") undertook to author the entire *razo* corpus and a great many of the *vidas*. The most famous and influential Italian troubadour was [Sordello](/wiki/Sordello "Sordello").[[11]](#cite_note-11)

The troubadours had a connection with the rise of a school of poetry in the [Kingdom of Sicily](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Sicily "Kingdom of Sicily"). In 1220 [Obs de Biguli](/wiki/Obs_de_Biguli "Obs de Biguli") was present as a "singer" at the coronation of the [Emperor Frederick II](/wiki/Emperor_Frederick_II "Emperor Frederick II"). [Guillem Augier Novella](/wiki/Guillem_Augier_Novella "Guillem Augier Novella") before 1230 and [Guilhem Figueira](/wiki/Guilhem_Figueira "Guilhem Figueira") thereafter were important Occitan poets at Frederick's court. The [Albigensian Crusade](/wiki/Albigensian_Crusade "Albigensian Crusade") had devastated [Languedoc](/wiki/Languedoc "Languedoc") and forced many troubadours of the area to flee to Italy, where an Italian tradition of papal criticism was begun.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

### Chivalric romance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Western_literature&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Chivalric romance")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/18/PalazzoTrinci010.jpg/250px-PalazzoTrinci010.jpg)](/wiki/File:PalazzoTrinci010.jpg)

[Jacobus de Voragine](/wiki/Jacobus_de_Voragine "Jacobus de Voragine") with the *[Golden Legend](/wiki/Golden_Legend "Golden Legend")* in his hand, fresco by [Ottaviano Nelli](/wiki/Ottaviano_Nelli "Ottaviano Nelli"), chapel of [Palazzo Trinci](/wiki/Palazzo_Trinci "Palazzo Trinci"), [Foligno](/wiki/Foligno "Foligno"), Italy

The *Historia de excidio Trojae*, attributed to [Dares Phrygius](/wiki/Dares_Phrygius "Dares Phrygius"), claimed to be an eyewitness account of the Trojan War. [Guido delle Colonne](/wiki/Guido_delle_Colonne "Guido delle Colonne") of [Messina](/wiki/Messina "Messina"), one of the [vernacular](/wiki/Vernacular "Vernacular") poets of the Sicilian school, composed the *[Historia destructionis Troiae](/wiki/Historia_destructionis_Troiae "Historia destructionis Troiae")*. In his poetry, Guido was an imitator of the [Provençals](/wiki/Provence "Provence"),[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBartoliOelsner1911898-7) but in this book he converted [Benoît de Sainte-Maure](/wiki/Beno%C3%AEt_de_Sainte-Maure "Benoît de Sainte-Maure")'s French romance into what sounded like serious Latin history.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

Much the same thing occurred with other great legends. [Quilichino of Spoleto](/wiki/Quilichino_of_Spoleto "Quilichino of Spoleto") wrote [couplets](/wiki/Couplet "Couplet") about the legend of [Alexander the Great](/wiki/Alexander_the_Great "Alexander the Great"). Europe was full of the legend of [King Arthur](/wiki/King_Arthur "King Arthur"), but the Italians contented themselves with translating and abridging French romances. [Jacobus de Voragine](/wiki/Jacobus_de_Voragine "Jacobus de Voragine"), while collecting his *[Golden Legend](/wiki/Golden_Legend "Golden Legend")* (1260), remained a historian.[[14]](#cite_note-14) Farfa, [Marsicano](/wiki/Marsicano "Marsicano"), and other scholars translated [Aristotle](/wiki/Aristotle "Aristotle"), the precepts of the school of [Salerno](/wiki/Salerno "Salerno"), and the travels of [Marco Polo](/wiki/Marco_Polo "Marco Polo"), linking the classics and the Renaissance.[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBartoliOelsner1911898-7)

At the same time, epic poetry was written in a mixed language, a dialect of Italian based on French: hybrid words exhibited a treatment of sounds according to the rules of both languages, had French roots with Italian endings, and were pronounced according to Italian or Latin rules. Examples include the *[chansons de geste](/wiki/Chanson_de_geste "Chanson de geste")*, *[Macaire](/wiki/Macaire "Macaire")*, the *[Entrée d'Espagne](/wiki/Entr%C3%A9e_d%27Espagne "Entrée d'Espagne")* written by Anonymous of [Padua](/wiki/Padua "Padua"), the *[Prise de Pampelune](/w/index.php?title=Prise_de_Pampelune&action=edit&redlink=1 "Prise de Pampelune (page does not exist)")*, written by [Niccolò of Verona](/w/index.php?title=Niccol%C3%B2_of_Verona&action=edit&redlink=1 "Niccolò of Verona (page does not exist)"), and others. All this preceded the appearance of purely Italian literature.[[15]](#cite_note-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16)

### Dante

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Western_literature&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Dante")]

Main article: [Dante Alighieri](/wiki/Dante_Alighieri "Dante Alighieri")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6f/Portrait_de_Dante.jpg/250px-Portrait_de_Dante.jpg)](/wiki/File:Portrait_de_Dante.jpg)

Profile portrait of [Dante Alighieri](/wiki/Dante_Alighieri "Dante Alighieri"), by [Sandro Botticelli](/wiki/Sandro_Botticelli "Sandro Botticelli")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f0/Inferno.jpg/250px-Inferno.jpg)](/wiki/File:Inferno.jpg)

First page of an early printed edition of Dante's *[Divine Comedy](/wiki/Divine_Comedy "Divine Comedy")*

[Dante Alighieri](/wiki/Dante_Alighieri "Dante Alighieri"), one of the greatest of Italian poets, also shows these lyrical tendencies.[[17]](#cite_note-17) In 1293 he wrote *[La Vita Nuova](/wiki/La_Vita_Nuova "La Vita Nuova")*, in which he idealizes love. It is a collection of poems to which Dante added narration and explication. Everything is sensual, aerial, and heavenly, and the real Beatrice is supplanted by an idealized vision of her, losing her human nature and becoming a representation of the divine.[[18]](#cite_note-18)
The *[Divine Comedy](/wiki/Divine_Comedy "Divine Comedy")* tells of the poet's travels through the three realms of the dead—[Hell](/wiki/Hell "Hell"), [Purgatory](/wiki/Purgatory "Purgatory"), and [Paradise](/wiki/Paradise "Paradise")—accompanied by the Latin poet [Virgil](/wiki/Virgil "Virgil"). An allegorical meaning hides under the literal one of this great epic. Dante, travelling through Hell, Purgatory, and Paradise, symbolizes mankind aiming at the double object of temporal and eternal happiness. The forest where the poet loses himself symbolizes sin.[[19]](#cite_note-19) The mountain illuminated by the sun is the universal monarchy.[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBartoliOelsner1911901-20) Envy is Florence, Pride is the house of France, and Avarice is the papal court. Virgil represents reason and the empire.[[21]](#cite_note-21) Beatrice is the symbol of the supernatural aid mankind must have to attain the supreme end, which is God.[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBartoliOelsner1911901-20)

The merit of the poem lies is the individual art of the poet, the classic art transfused for the first time into a Romance form. Whether he describes nature, analyses passions, curses the vices or sings hymns to the virtues, Dante is notable for the grandeur and delicacy of his art. He took the materials for his poem from [theology](/wiki/Theology "Theology"), philosophy, history, and mythology, but especially from his own passions, from hatred and love.[[22]](#cite_note-22) The *Divine Comedy* ranks among the finest works of [world literature](/wiki/World_literature "World literature").[[23]](#cite_note-23)

### Petrarch

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Western_literature&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Petrarch")]

Main article: [Petrarch](/wiki/Petrarch "Petrarch")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/59/Francesco_Petrarca00.jpg/250px-Francesco_Petrarca00.jpg)](/wiki/File:Francesco_Petrarca00.jpg)

[Petrarch](/wiki/Petrarch "Petrarch")

Petrarch was the first [humanist](/wiki/Humanism "Humanism"), and he was at the same time the first modern lyric poet.[[24]](#cite_note-24)[[25]](#cite_note-25) His career was long and tempestuous. He lived for many years at [Avignon](/wiki/Avignon "Avignon"), cursing the corruption of the papal court; he travelled through nearly the whole of Europe; he corresponded with emperors and popes, and he was considered the most important writer of his time.[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBartoliOelsner1911901-20) Petrarch's lyric verse is quite different, not only from that of the Provençal [troubadours](/wiki/Troubadour "Troubadour") and the Italian poets before him, but also from the lyrics of Dante.[[26]](#cite_note-26) Petrarch is a psychological poet, who examines all his feelings and renders them with an art of exquisite sweetness. The lyrics of Petrarch are no longer transcendental like Dante's, but keep entirely within human limits.

The *Canzoniere* includes a few political poems, one supposed to be addressed to [Cola di Rienzi](/wiki/Cola_di_Rienzi "Cola di Rienzi") and several sonnets against the court of Avignon. These are remarkable for their vigour of feeling, and also for showing that, compared to Dante, Petrarch had a sense of a broader Italian consciousness.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

### Boccaccio

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Western_literature&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Boccaccio")]

Main article: [Giovanni Boccaccio](/wiki/Giovanni_Boccaccio "Giovanni Boccaccio")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/29/Andrea_del_Castagno_Giovanni_Boccaccio_c_1450.jpg/250px-Andrea_del_Castagno_Giovanni_Boccaccio_c_1450.jpg)](/wiki/File:Andrea_del_Castagno_Giovanni_Boccaccio_c_1450.jpg)

[Giovanni Boccaccio](/wiki/Giovanni_Boccaccio "Giovanni Boccaccio") presenting the *[Decamerone](/wiki/Decameron "Decameron")*, depicted by [Andrea del Castagno](/wiki/Andrea_del_Castagno "Andrea del Castagno") in a fresco preserved in the [Uffizi](/wiki/Uffizi "Uffizi")

[Giovanni Boccaccio](/wiki/Giovanni_Boccaccio "Giovanni Boccaccio") had the same enthusiastic love of antiquity and the same worship for the new Italian literature as Petrarch.[[28]](#cite_note-boccaccio-28) He was the first to put together a Latin translation of the *[Iliad](/wiki/Iliad "Iliad")* and, in 1375, the *[Odyssey](/wiki/Odyssey "Odyssey")*. His classical learning was shown in the work *De genealogia deorum*; as [A. H. Heeren](/wiki/A._H._Heeren "A. H. Heeren") said, it is an encyclopaedia of mythological knowledge; and it was the precursor of the [humanist](/wiki/Humanism "Humanism") movement of the 15th century.[[29]](#cite_note-29) Boccaccio was also the first historian of women in his *[De mulieribus claris](/wiki/De_mulieribus_claris "De mulieribus claris")*, and the first to tell the story of the great unfortunates in his *[De casibus virorum illustrium](/wiki/De_casibus_virorum_illustrium "De casibus virorum illustrium")*. He continued and perfected former geographical investigations in his *De montibus, silvis, fontibus, lacubus, fluminibus, stagnis, et paludibus, et de nominibus maris*, for which he made use of [Vibius Sequester](/wiki/Vibius_Sequester "Vibius Sequester").

He did not invent the [octave stanza](/wiki/Octave_stanza "Octave stanza"), but was the first to use it in a work of length and artistic merit, his *[Teseide](/w/index.php?title=Teseide&action=edit&redlink=1 "Teseide (page does not exist)")*, the oldest Italian romantic poem. The *[Filostrato](/wiki/Filostrato "Filostrato")* relates the loves of Troiolo and Griseida ([Troilus and Cressida](/wiki/Troilus_and_Cressida "Troilus and Cressida")). The *[Ninfale fiesolano](/w/index.php?title=Ninfale_fiesolano&action=edit&redlink=1 "Ninfale fiesolano (page does not exist)")* tells the love story of the nymph Mesola and the shepherd Africo. The *[Amorosa Visione](/w/index.php?title=Amorosa_Visione&action=edit&redlink=1 "Amorosa Visione (page does not exist)")*, a poem in triplets, doubtless owed its origin to the *Divine Comedy*. The *[Ameto](/w/index.php?title=Ameto&action=edit&redlink=1 "Ameto (page does not exist)")* is a mixture of prose and poetry, and is the first Italian pastoral romance.[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBartoliOelsner1911902-30)
Boccaccio became famous principally for the Italian work, *[Decamerone](/wiki/Decameron "Decameron")*, a collection of a hundred novels, related by a party of men and women who retired to a villa near Florence to escape the [plague](/wiki/Black_Death "Black Death") in 1348. Novel writing, so abundant in the preceding centuries, especially in France, now for the first time assumed an artistic shape.[[31]](#cite_note-31) The style of Boccaccio tends to the imitation of Latin, but in him, prose first took the form of elaborated art. Over and above this, in the *Decamerone*, Boccaccio is a delineator of character and an observer of passions. Much has been written about the sources of the novels of the *Decamerone*. Probably Boccaccio made use both of written and of oral sources.[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBartoliOelsner1911902-30)

## 15th century: Renaissance humanism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Western_literature&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: 15th century: Renaissance humanism")]

See also: [Quattrocento](/wiki/Quattrocento "Quattrocento")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/85/Walker_Art_Gallery%2C_Leonard_Bruni.jpg/250px-Walker_Art_Gallery%2C_Leonard_Bruni.jpg)](/wiki/File:Walker_Art_Gallery,_Leonard_Bruni.jpg)

[Leonardo Bruni](/wiki/Leonardo_Bruni "Leonardo Bruni")

Renaissance humanism developed during the 14th and the beginning of the 15th centuries, and was a response to the challenge of Mediæval scholastic education, emphasizing practical, pre-professional and -scientific studies. [Scholasticism](/wiki/Scholasticism "Scholasticism") focused on preparing men to be doctors, lawyers or professional theologians, and was taught from approved textbooks in logic, natural philosophy, medicine, law and theology.[[32]](#cite_note-32) The main centers of humanism were [Florence](/wiki/Florence "Florence") and [Naples](/wiki/Naples "Naples").[[33]](#cite_note-33)

Rather than train professionals in jargon and strict practice, humanists sought to create a citizenry (including, sometimes, women) able to speak and write with eloquence and clarity. This was to be accomplished through the study of the *[studia humanitatis](/wiki/Humanitas "Humanitas")*, today known as the [humanities](/wiki/Humanities "Humanities"): grammar, rhetoric, history, poetry and moral philosophy.[[34]](#cite_note-34) Early humanists, such as [Petrarch](/wiki/Petrarch "Petrarch"), [Coluccio Salutati](/wiki/Coluccio_Salutati "Coluccio Salutati") and [Leonardo Bruni](/wiki/Leonardo_Bruni "Leonardo Bruni"), were great collectors of antique manuscripts.

In Italy, the humanist educational program won rapid acceptance and, by the mid-15th century, many of the upper classes had received humanist educations. There were five 15th century Humanist Popes,[[35]](#cite_note-35) one of whom, [Aeneas Silvius Piccolomini](/wiki/Aeneas_Silvius_Piccolomini "Aeneas Silvius Piccolomini") (Pius II), was a prolific author and wrote a treatise on "The Education of Boys".[[36]](#cite_note-36)

### Literature in the Florence of the Medici

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Western_literature&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Literature in the Florence of the Medici")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/10/Lorenzo_de_Medici.jpg/250px-Lorenzo_de_Medici.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lorenzo_de_Medici.jpg)

Portrait of [Lorenzo de' Medici](/wiki/Lorenzo_de%27_Medici "Lorenzo de' Medici") by [Agnolo Bronzino](/wiki/Agnolo_Bronzino "Agnolo Bronzino") at the [Uffizi](/wiki/Uffizi "Uffizi"), [Florence](/wiki/Florence "Florence")

[Leone Battista Alberti](/wiki/Leone_Battista_Alberti "Leone Battista Alberti"), the learned Greek and Latin scholar, wrote in the vernacular, and [Vespasiano da Bisticci](/wiki/Vespasiano_da_Bisticci "Vespasiano da Bisticci"), while he was constantly absorbed in Greek and Latin manuscripts, wrote the *Vite di uomini illustri*, valuable for their historical contents, and rivalling the best works of the 14th century in their candour and simplicity. [Andrea da Barberino](/wiki/Andrea_da_Barberino "Andrea da Barberino") wrote the beautiful prose of the *Reali di Francia*, giving a coloring of *romanità* to the chivalrous romances. [Belcari](/w/index.php?title=Feo_Belcari&action=edit&redlink=1 "Feo Belcari (page does not exist)") and [Girolamo Benivieni](/wiki/Girolamo_Benivieni "Girolamo Benivieni") returned to the mystic idealism of earlier times.[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBartoliOelsner1911904-37)

But it is in [Cosimo de' Medici](/wiki/Cosimo_de%27_Medici "Cosimo de' Medici") and [Lorenzo de' Medici](/wiki/Lorenzo_de%27_Medici "Lorenzo de' Medici"), from 1430 to 1492, that the influence of Florence on the Renaissance is particularly seen.[[38]](#cite_note-38) Lorenzo de' Medici gave to his poetry the colors of the most pronounced realism as well as of the loftiest idealism, who passes from the Platonic [sonnet](/wiki/Sonnet "Sonnet") to the impassioned triplets of the *Amori di Venere*, from the grandiosity of the *Salve to Nencia* and to Beoni, from the *[Canto carnascialesco](/wiki/Canto_carnascialesco "Canto carnascialesco")* to the *lauda*.[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBartoliOelsner1911904–905-39)

Next to Lorenzo comes [Poliziano](/wiki/Poliziano "Poliziano"), who also united, and with greater art, the ancient and the modern, the popular and the classical style. In his *Rispetti* and in his *Ballate* the freshness of imagery and the plasticity of form are inimitable.[[40]](#cite_note-40) A great Greek scholar, Poliziano wrote Italian verses with dazzling colours; the purest elegance of the Greek sources pervaded his art in all its varieties, in the *Orfeo* as well as the *Stanze per la giostra*.[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBartoliOelsner1911905-41)

A completely new style of poetry arose, the *Canto carnascialesco*. These were a type of choral songs, which were accompanied by symbolic masquerades, common in Florence at the carnival. They were written in a metre like that of the *ballate*; and for the most part, they were put into the mouth of a party of workmen and tradesmen, who, with not very chaste allusions, sang the praises of their art. These triumphs and masquerades were directed by Lorenzo himself. In the evening, there set out into the city large companies on horseback, playing and singing these songs. There are some by Lorenzo himself, which surpass all the others in their mastery of art. That entitled *Bacco ed Arianna* is the most famous.[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBartoliOelsner1911905-41)[[42]](#cite_note-42)

## 16th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Western_literature&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: 16th century")]

### Renaissance and Reformation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Western_literature&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Renaissance and Reformation")]

#### England

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Western_literature&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: England")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/21/Book_of_Common_Prayer%2C_1549_%282%29.jpg/250px-Book_of_Common_Prayer%2C_1549_%282%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Book_of_Common_Prayer,_1549_(2).jpg)

The first *Book of Common Prayer*, published 1549

[Early modern England](/wiki/Early_modern_Britain "Early modern Britain") was the time of reformation, in which a "[Protestant](/wiki/Protestantism "Protestantism") aesthetic" was developed, while the [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England") attempted to separate their notoriety with the [Pope](/wiki/Pope "Pope") and move away from the teachings of the [Roman Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church").[[2]](#cite_note-:13-2) [Johannine](/wiki/Johannine_literature "Johannine literature") literature, being "hymnic, densely troped and symbolic, structured, inspired",[[2]](#cite_note-:13-2) became the inspiration for many poets of the period. A group of poets bloomed from this reformation, the rejection of the Pope and moving away from Roman Catholic Church.[[2]](#cite_note-:13-2) Amongst these were the most significant [John Donne](/wiki/John_Donne "John Donne"), [George Herbert](/wiki/George_Herbert "George Herbert") and [Thomas Traherne](/wiki/Thomas_Traherne "Thomas Traherne"), and constituted a group of poets known as "revelatory poetics".[[2]](#cite_note-:13-2)

The narrative which grew more prominent in English literature due to this movement towards Johannine theology incorporated an increase of spiritual themes, with "supernatural forces" and an "enchantment narrative" guiding the writings of the time.[[2]](#cite_note-:13-2) [Johannine](/wiki/Johannine_literature "Johannine literature") theology focused on the "divine" nature of [Christ](/wiki/Jesus "Jesus") and disregards the materialistic and human aspect acknowledged in Catholic texts.[[43]](#cite_note-:14-43) It has been argued that the writings of Saint John the Evangelist, which was considered an integral part of Johannine theology, coincided with [Pauline theology](/wiki/Pauline_Christianity "Pauline Christianity") during the early modern era to hold influence over the English literature of the time.[[43]](#cite_note-:14-43) Author Paul Cefalu claims this form of "high Christology" was seen in the writings of John Donne, when he states that the "Gospel of Saint John containes all Divinity".[[44]](#cite_note-44) However, it is argued by author P. M. Oliver that the theology which was indoctrinated in the poetry of revelatory poets including John Donne was expanded on and created by the poets themselves.[[45]](#cite_note-45)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/98/Obras_de_Bosc%C3%A1n_y_Garcilaso_de_la_Vega.jpeg/250px-Obras_de_Bosc%C3%A1n_y_Garcilaso_de_la_Vega.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Obras_de_Bosc%C3%A1n_y_Garcilaso_de_la_Vega.jpeg)

Cover of *The works of Boscán and Garcilaso de la Vega in 4 books*, published in 1543

Prominent forms of literature which shaped and contributed to this era of Reformation include significantly structured prose and poetry, including the [Spenserian stanza](/wiki/Spenserian_stanza "Spenserian stanza");[[46]](#cite_note-:15-46) the [sonnet](/wiki/Sonnet "Sonnet"), which is a form of poem easily distinguishable by its fourteen-line form with a structured rhyme format;[[46]](#cite_note-:15-46) and the pastoral mode, a genre of literature which is significantly attributed to English poet [Edmund Spenser](/wiki/Edmund_Spenser "Edmund Spenser"), who created collections of poetry which portrays an idealistic version of rural living.[[47]](#cite_note-:16-47) Spenser has been "dubbed 'the English [Virgil](/wiki/Virgil "Virgil")'" due to his influence on this particular genre.[[47]](#cite_note-:16-47)

Significant texts from 16th-century early modern England were primarily religious in context and include:

* The Great Bible, edited by Myles Coverdale.
* The first *[Book of Common Prayer](/wiki/Book_of_Common_Prayer_(1549) "Book of Common Prayer (1549)")*, published on January 15, 1549, after being accepted by the House of Lords.[[48]](#cite_note-:17-48) The book, due to the political and authoritative changes of the time of the reformation, attempted to provide a "compromise" between Protestant and Roman Catholic beliefs.[[48]](#cite_note-:17-48) The author of the book, [Thomas Cranmer](/wiki/Thomas_Cranmer "Thomas Cranmer"), assisted in creating a standard version of the modern English language.[[49]](#cite_note-49)

#### Italy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Western_literature&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Italy")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e2/Portrait_of_Niccol%C3%B2_Machiavelli_by_Santi_di_Tito.jpg/250px-Portrait_of_Niccol%C3%B2_Machiavelli_by_Santi_di_Tito.jpg)](/wiki/File:Portrait_of_Niccol%C3%B2_Machiavelli_by_Santi_di_Tito.jpg)

Portrait of [Niccolò Machiavelli](/wiki/Niccol%C3%B2_Machiavelli "Niccolò Machiavelli") by [Santi di Tito](/wiki/Santi_di_Tito "Santi di Tito")

[Niccolò Machiavelli](/wiki/Niccol%C3%B2_Machiavelli "Niccolò Machiavelli") and [Francesco Guicciardini](/wiki/Francesco_Guicciardini "Francesco Guicciardini") were the chief originators of the science of history.[[50]](#cite_note-50) Machiavelli's principal works are the *Istorie fiorentine*, the *Discorsi sulla prima deca di Tito Livio*, the *Arte della guerra* and the *Principe*. His merit consists in having emphasized the experimental side of the study of political action by having observed facts, studied histories and drawn principles from them. His history is sometimes inexact in facts; it is rather a political than a historical work.[[51]](#cite_note-51)

Guicciardini was very observant and endeavoured to reduce his observations to a science.[[52]](#cite_note-52) His *Storia d'Italia*, which extends from the death of [Lorenzo de' Medici](/wiki/Lorenzo_de%27_Medici "Lorenzo de' Medici") to 1534, is full of political wisdom, is skillfully arranged in its parts, gives a lively picture of the character of the persons it treats of, and is written in a grand style. Machiavelli and Guicciardini may be considered distinguished historians as well as originators of the science of history founded on observation.[[53]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBartoliOelsner1911906-53)[[54]](#cite_note-54)

Inferior to them were [Jacopo Nardi](/wiki/Jacopo_Nardi "Jacopo Nardi") (a just and faithful historian and a virtuous man, who defended the rights of Florence against the Medici before Charles V), [Benedetto Varchi](/wiki/Benedetto_Varchi "Benedetto Varchi"), [Giambattista Adriani](/wiki/Giambattista_Adriani "Giambattista Adriani"), [Bernardo Segni](/w/index.php?title=Bernardo_Segni&action=edit&redlink=1 "Bernardo Segni (page does not exist)"), and, outside Tuscany, [Camillo Porzio](/wiki/Camillo_Porzio "Camillo Porzio"), who related the *Congiura de baroni* and the history of Italy from 1547 to 1552; [Angelo di Costanzo](/wiki/Angelo_di_Costanzo "Angelo di Costanzo"), [Pietro Bembo](/wiki/Pietro_Bembo "Pietro Bembo"), [Paolo Paruta](/wiki/Paolo_Paruta "Paolo Paruta"), and others.[[53]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBartoliOelsner1911906-53)

[Ludovico Ariosto](/wiki/Ludovico_Ariosto "Ludovico Ariosto")'s *[Orlando furioso](/wiki/Orlando_furioso "Orlando furioso")* was a continuation of Boiardo's *Innamorato*. His characteristic is that he assimilated the romance of chivalry into the style and models of classicism. Romantic Ariosto was an artist only for the love of his art;[[53]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBartoliOelsner1911906-53) his epic.[[55]](#cite_note-Ariosto-55)

### Golden Age

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Western_literature&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Golden Age")]

#### Spain

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Western_literature&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Spain")]

The [Spanish Golden Age](/wiki/Spanish_Golden_Age "Spanish Golden Age") spanned over the course of the 16th century and was a time of development and acceleration in the arts and literature in Spain. This acceleration of [poetry](/wiki/Poetry "Poetry"), [drama](/wiki/Drama "Drama") and [prose](/wiki/Prose "Prose") forms of literature was partly due to the increase in contact that Spain gained to other European nations including Italy.[[56]](#cite_note-:0-56) During this time, a prominent Spanish poet arose named [Garcilaso de la Vega](/wiki/Garcilaso_de_la_Vega_(poet) "Garcilaso de la Vega (poet)"). He utilised [literary devices](/wiki/List_of_narrative_techniques "List of narrative techniques") seen in foreign nations within his work, and was able to, therefore, replace the [stanza](/wiki/Stanza "Stanza") forms originally used in Spain with Italian meters and stanza forms.[[56]](#cite_note-:0-56) The poet was influenced by [Petrarchan](/wiki/Petrarchan_sonnet "Petrarchan sonnet") imagery and the works of [Virgil](/wiki/Virgil "Virgil"), and was used as inspiration by subsequent poets of the time.[[56]](#cite_note-:0-56) Garcilaso integrated a variety of mythological allusions into his works, in which he took inspiration from the [Italian Renaissance](/wiki/Italian_Renaissance "Italian Renaissance") of the mid-16th century.[[57]](#cite_note-57)

## 17th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Western_literature&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: 17th century")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/01/Bible.malmesbury.arp.jpg/330px-Bible.malmesbury.arp.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bible.malmesbury.arp.jpg)

The Bible, displayed in Malmesbury Abbey, Wiltshire, England

[Prose](/wiki/Prose "Prose") and poetic literature within western regions, most prominently in England during the early [modern era](/wiki/Modern_era "Modern era"), had a distinct [Biblical](/wiki/Bible "Bible") influence[[3]](#cite_note-:1-3) which only began to be rejected during the Enlightenment period of the 18th century.[[58]](#cite_note-:4-58) European poetry during the 17th century tended to meditate on or reference the scriptures and teachings of the [Bible](/wiki/Bible "Bible"), an example being orator [George Herbert](/wiki/George_Herbert "George Herbert")'s "The Holy Scriptures (II)", in which Herbert relies heavily on biblical ligatures to create his [sonnets](/wiki/Sonnet "Sonnet").[[3]](#cite_note-:1-3)

### Jacobean era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Western_literature&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Jacobean era")]

#### England

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Western_literature&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: England")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/74/John_Donne_BBC_News.jpg/250px-John_Donne_BBC_News.jpg)](/wiki/File:John_Donne_BBC_News.jpg)

Portrait of English metaphysical John Donne

The [Jacobean](/wiki/Jacobean_era "Jacobean era") period of 17th-century England gave birth to a group of [metaphysic](/wiki/Metaphysics "Metaphysics") literary figures,[[59]](#cite_note-:20-59) metaphysical referring to a branch of philosophy which tries to bring meaning to and explain reality using broader and larger concepts.[[59]](#cite_note-:20-59) In order to do this, the use of literary features including conceits was common, in which the writer makes obscure comparisons in order to convey a message or persuade a point.[[59]](#cite_note-:20-59)

The term [metaphysics](/wiki/Metaphysics "Metaphysics") was coined by poet [John Dryden](/wiki/John_Dryden "John Dryden"), and during 1779 its meaning was extended to represent a group of poets of the time, then called "[metaphysical poets](/wiki/Metaphysical_poets "Metaphysical poets")".[[60]](#cite_note-:2-60) Major poets of the time included [John Donne](/wiki/John_Donne "John Donne"), [Andrew Marvell](/wiki/Andrew_Marvell "Andrew Marvell") and [George Herbert](/wiki/George_Herbert "George Herbert").[[60]](#cite_note-:2-60) These poets used wit and high intellectual standards while drawing from nature to reveal insights about emotion and rejected the romantic attributes of the [Elizabethan period](/wiki/Elizabethan_era "Elizabethan era") to birth a more analytical and introspective form of writing.[[60]](#cite_note-:2-60) A common literary device during the 17th century was the use of metaphysical [conceits](/wiki/Conceit "Conceit"), in which the poet uses "unorthodox language" to describe a relatable concept. It is beneficial when trying to bring light to concepts that are difficult to explain with more common imagery.[[61]](#cite_note-:3-61)

[John Donne](/wiki/John_Donne "John Donne") was a prominent metaphysical poet of the 17th century. Donne's poetry explored the pleasures of life through strong use of conceits and emotive language. Donne adopted a more simplistic vernacular compared to the common [Petrarchan](/wiki/Petrarchan_sonnet "Petrarchan sonnet") diction, with imagery derived mainly from God.[[61]](#cite_note-:3-61) Donne was known for the metaphysical [conceits](/wiki/Conceit "Conceit") integrated in his poetry. He used themes of religion, death and love to inspire the conceits he constructed. A famous conceit is observed in his well-known poem "[The Flea](/wiki/The_Flea_(poem) "The Flea (poem)")" in which the flea is utilised to describe the bond between Donne and his lover, explaining how just as multiple bloods are within one flea, their bond is inseparable.[[61]](#cite_note-:3-61)

### Italy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Western_literature&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Italy")]

The [Treaty of Cateau-Cambrésis](/wiki/Treaty_of_Cateau-Cambr%C3%A9sis "Treaty of Cateau-Cambrésis") in 1559 ushered in centuries of foreign domination over Italy.[[62]](#cite_note-62) This period is known in the history of Italian literature as the *Secentismo*.[[63]](#cite_note-63) Its writers deployed complex, far-fetched comparisons, paradoxes, and paralogical statements (*acutezze*) in order to exhibit the writer's genius and ingenuity (*ingegno*), and provoke wonder (*meraviglia*) in the reader.[[64]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBartoliOelsner1911908-64)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1b/Frans_Pourbus_the_Younger_-_Portrait_of_Giovanni_Battista_Marino.jpg/250px-Frans_Pourbus_the_Younger_-_Portrait_of_Giovanni_Battista_Marino.jpg)](/wiki/File:Frans_Pourbus_the_Younger_-_Portrait_of_Giovanni_Battista_Marino.jpg)

Portrait of [Giambattista Marino](/wiki/Giambattista_Marino "Giambattista Marino") by [Frans Pourbus the Younger](/wiki/Frans_Pourbus_the_Younger "Frans Pourbus the Younger")

At the head of the school of the *Secentisti*[[65]](#cite_note-65) was [Giambattista Marino](/wiki/Giambattista_Marino "Giambattista Marino"), especially known for his epic poem, *L'Adone*. Marino himself, as he declared in the Preface to *La lira*, wished to be a new leader and model for other poets. Second, he wished to surprise and shock the reader through the marvellous (*meraviglioso*) and the unusual (*peregrino*). The qualities he and his followers most valued were *ingegno* and *acutezza*, as demonstrated through far-fetched metaphors and [conceits](/wiki/Conceit "Conceit"), often ones that would assault the reader's senses. This meant being ready, in fact eager, to break literary rules and precepts. Marino and his followers mixed tradition and innovation: they worked with existing poetic forms, notably the [sonnet](/wiki/Sonnet "Sonnet"), the [sestina](/wiki/Sestina "Sestina"), the [canzone](/wiki/Canzone "Canzone"), the [madrigal](/wiki/Madrigal "Madrigal"), and less frequently the [ottava rima](/wiki/Ottava_rima "Ottava rima"), but developed new, more fluid structures and line lengths. They also treated hallowed themes (love, woman, nature), but they made the senses and sensuality the dominant element. The passions, which had attracted the attention of Paduan writers and theorists in the mid-16th century as well as of Tasso, take centre stage, and are depicted in extreme forms in representations of subjects such as martyrdom, sacrifice, heroic grandeur, and abysmal existential fear. The Marinists also take up new themes—notably the visual and musical arts and indoor scenes—with a new repertoire of references embracing modern scientific advances, other specialized branches of knowledge, and exotic locations and animals. There are similarities with Tasso, but the balance between form and content in Tasso is deliberately unbalanced by Marino and his followers, who very often forget all concerns about unity in their poems (witness the *Adone*). The most striking difference, however, is the intensified role of [metaphor](/wiki/Metaphor "Metaphor"). Marino and his followers looked for metaphors that would arrest the reader by suggesting a likeness between two apparently disparate things, thus producing startling metamorphoses, conceits (*concetti*), and far-fetched images that send sparks flying as they create a friction between two apparently diverse objects. The extent to which this new metaphorical freedom reveals a new world is still open to critical debate. In some ways it seems to make poetry a form of intellectual game or puzzle; in others it suggests new ways of perceiving and describing reality, parallel to the mathematical measures employed by Galileo and his followers in the experimental sciences.

Almost all the poets of the 17th century were more or less influenced by Marinism. Many *secentisti* felt the influence of another poet, [Gabriello Chiabrera](/wiki/Gabriello_Chiabrera "Gabriello Chiabrera"). Enamoured of the Greeks, he made new metres, especially in imitation of [Pindar](/wiki/Pindar "Pindar"),[[66]](#cite_note-66) treating of religious, moral, historical, and amatory subjects. [Carlo Alessandro Guidi](/wiki/Carlo_Alessandro_Guidi "Carlo Alessandro Guidi") was the chief representative of an early Pindarizing current based on imitation of Chiabrera as second only to Petrarch in Italian poetry. He was extolled by both Gravina and Crescimbeni, who edited his poetry (1726), and imitated by Parini. Alfieri attributed his own self-discovery to the power of Guidi's verse. [Fulvio Testi](/wiki/Fulvio_Testi "Fulvio Testi") was another major exponent of the Hellenizing strand of Baroque classicism, combining Horatianism with the imitation of Anacreon and Pindar. His most important and interesting writings are not, however, his lyrics (only collected in 1653), but his extensive correspondence, which is a major document of Baroque politics and letters.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3c/S_Eustachio_-_piazza_s_Agostino_Arcadia_e_Angelica_1150281.jpg/250px-S_Eustachio_-_piazza_s_Agostino_Arcadia_e_Angelica_1150281.jpg)](/wiki/File:S_Eustachio_-_piazza_s_Agostino_Arcadia_e_Angelica_1150281.jpg)

The [Accademia dell' Arcadia](/wiki/Pontifical_Academy_of_Arcadia "Pontifical Academy of Arcadia") headquarters in Piazza Sant'Agostino in Rome

Marino's work, with its sensual metaphorical language and its non-epic structure and morality, stirred up a debate over the rival claims of classical purity and sobriety on the one hand and the excesses of marinism on the other. The debate went on until it was finally decided in favour of the classical by the [Accademia dell'Arcadia](/wiki/Accademia_dell%27Arcadia "Accademia dell'Arcadia"), whose view of the matter prevailed in Italian criticism well into the 20th century.[[67]](#cite_note-67) The Accademia dell'Arcadia was founded by [Giovanni Mario Crescimbeni](/wiki/Giovanni_Mario_Crescimbeni "Giovanni Mario Crescimbeni") and [Gian Vincenzo Gravina](/wiki/Gian_Vincenzo_Gravina "Gian Vincenzo Gravina") in 1690. The *Arcadia* was so called because its chief aim was to imitate the simplicity of the ancient shepherds who were supposed to have lived in [Arcadia](/wiki/Arcadia_(utopia) "Arcadia (utopia)") in the golden age. The poems of the Arcadians are made up of [sonnets](/wiki/Sonnet "Sonnet"), [madrigals](/wiki/Madrigal_(music) "Madrigal (music)"), *[canzonette](/wiki/Canzonette "Canzonette")* and [blank verse](/wiki/Blank_verse "Blank verse"). The one who most distinguished himself among the sonneteers was [Felice Zappi](/wiki/Felice_Zappi "Felice Zappi"). Among the authors of songs, [Paolo Rolli](/wiki/Paolo_Rolli "Paolo Rolli") was illustrious. [Carlo Innocenzo Frugoni](/wiki/Carlo_Innocenzo_Frugoni "Carlo Innocenzo Frugoni") was the best known.[[64]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBartoliOelsner1911908-64) The members of the Arcadia were almost exclusively men, but at least one woman, [Maria Antonia Scalera Stellini](/wiki/Maria_Antonia_Scalera_Stellini "Maria Antonia Scalera Stellini"), was elected on poetical merits.[[68]](#cite_note-68) [Vincenzo da Filicaja](/wiki/Vincenzo_da_Filicaja "Vincenzo da Filicaja") had a lyric talent, particularly in the songs about [Vienna](/wiki/Vienna "Vienna") besieged by the [Turks](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire").[[64]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBartoliOelsner1911908-64)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5d/Portrait_of_Tommaso_Campanella_%28by_Francesco_Cozza%29.jpg/250px-Portrait_of_Tommaso_Campanella_%28by_Francesco_Cozza%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Portrait_of_Tommaso_Campanella_(by_Francesco_Cozza).jpg)

Portrait of [Tommaso Campanella](/wiki/Tommaso_Campanella "Tommaso Campanella") by [Francesco Cozza](/wiki/Francesco_Cozza_(painter) "Francesco Cozza (painter)")

The philosopher, theologian, astrologer, and poet [Tommaso Campanella](/wiki/Tommaso_Campanella "Tommaso Campanella") is an interesting albeit isolated figure in 17th century Italian literature. His *Poesie*, published in 1622, consist of eighty-nine poems in various metrical forms. Some are autobiographical, but all are stamped with a seriousness and directness which bypasses the literary fashions of his day. He wrote in Latin on dialectics, rhetoric, poetics, and historiography, as well as the Italian *Del senso delle cose e della magia*, composed in 1604 and published in 1620. In this fascinating work, influenced by the teachings of [Bernardino Telesio](/wiki/Bernardino_Telesio "Bernardino Telesio"), Campanella imagines the world as a living statue of God, in which all aspects of reality have meaning and sense. With its animism and sensuality this vision foreshadows in many ways the views of [Daniello Bartoli](/wiki/Daniello_Bartoli "Daniello Bartoli") and Tesauro.[[69]](#cite_note-69) Campanella's theological work, closely connected with his philosophical writings, includes the *Atheismus triumphatus* and the thirty-volume *Theologia* (1613–24). His most famous work, and the one that brings together all his interests, is *La città del sole*, first drafted in 1602 in Italian and then later translated into Latin in 1613 and 1631. In it a Genoese sailor from [Christopher Columbus](/wiki/Christopher_Columbus "Christopher Columbus")' crew describes the ideal state of the City of the Sun ruled over in both temporal and spiritual matters by the Prince Priest, called Sun or Metaphysician. Under him there are three ministers: Power (concerned with war and peace), Wisdom (concerned with science and art, all written down in one book), and Love (concerned with procreation and education of the citizens of the Sun). The life of the citizens is based on a system of [communism](/wiki/Communism "Communism"): all property is held publicly, there are no families, no rights of inheritance, no marriage, and sexual relations are regulated by the state. Everyone has his or her function in the society, and certain duties are required of all citizens. Education is the perfect training of the mind and the body, and it is radically opposed to the bookish and academic culture of Renaissance Italy: the objects of study should be not 'dead things' but nature and the mathematical and physical laws that govern the physical world. There are links here with the burgeoning modernism of the *[Querelle des anciens et des modernes](/wiki/Quarrel_of_the_Ancients_and_the_Moderns "Quarrel of the Ancients and the Moderns")*, and with the methods and scientific aspirations of Galileo, whom Campanella defended in writing in 1616.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c7/Justus_Sustermans_-_Portrait_of_Galileo_Galilei_%28Uffizi%29.jpg/250px-Justus_Sustermans_-_Portrait_of_Galileo_Galilei_%28Uffizi%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Justus_Sustermans_-_Portrait_of_Galileo_Galilei_(Uffizi).jpg)

Portrait of [Galileo Galilei](/wiki/Galileo_Galilei "Galileo Galilei") by [Justus Sustermans](/wiki/Justus_Sustermans "Justus Sustermans")

The [Lyncean Academy](/wiki/Lyncean_Academy "Lyncean Academy"), the first and most famous of the scientific academies in Italy, was founded in 1603 in Rome by [Federico Cesi](/wiki/Federico_Cesi "Federico Cesi"). The academy dedicated its activities to the study of the natural and mathematical sciences and to the use of the experimental method associated with Galileo. The European dimension of the academy was characteristic of the founders' foresight and perspective: elections were made of foreign corresponding members, a practice that continues to this day. Members included [Claudio Achillini](/wiki/Claudio_Achillini "Claudio Achillini"), [Pietro Della Valle](/wiki/Pietro_Della_Valle "Pietro Della Valle"), Galileo (from 1611), [Francesco Sforza Pallavicino](/wiki/Francesco_Sforza_Pallavicino "Francesco Sforza Pallavicino"), [Giambattista Della Porta](/wiki/Giambattista_della_Porta "Giambattista della Porta") (from 1610 ), and [Filippo Salviati](/wiki/Filippo_Salviati "Filippo Salviati"). Their work involved the large-scale publishing of scientific results based on direct observation, including Galileo's work on the moon's surface (1610) and his *[Assayer](/wiki/The_Assayer "The Assayer")* (1623). The academy defended Galileo at his trial in 1616, and played a crucial role in the early diffusion and promotion of his method.

The successor of the Lynceans was the [Accademia del Cimento](/wiki/Accademia_del_Cimento "Accademia del Cimento"), founded in Florence in 1657. Never as organized as the Lynceans had been, it began as a meeting of disciples of Galileo, all of whom were interested in the progress of the experimental sciences. Official status came in 1657, when cardinal [Leopoldo de' Medici](/wiki/Leopoldo_de%27_Medici "Leopoldo de' Medici") sponsored the academy's foundation. With the motto 'provando e riprovando', the members, including [Carlo Roberto Dati](/wiki/Carlo_Roberto_Dati "Carlo Roberto Dati"), [Lorenzo Magalotti](/wiki/Lorenzo_Magalotti "Lorenzo Magalotti"), and [Vincenzo Viviani](/wiki/Vincenzo_Viviani "Vincenzo Viviani"), set seriously about their work. Unlike Galileo, who tackled large-scale issues, the Cimento worked on a smaller scale. One of the legacies of the Cimento is the elegant Italian prose, capable of describing things accurately, that characterizes the *Saggi di naturali esperienze* edited by Magalotti and published in 1667.

[Galileo](/wiki/Galileo_Galilei "Galileo Galilei") occupied a conspicuous place in the history of letters. A devoted student of Ariosto, he seemed to transfuse into his prose the qualities of that great poet: clear and frank freedom of expression, precision and ease, and at the same time elegance.[[64]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBartoliOelsner1911908-64)[[70]](#cite_note-70) [Paganino Bonafede](/w/index.php?title=Paganino_Bonafede&action=edit&redlink=1 "Paganino Bonafede (page does not exist)") in the *Tesoro dei rustici* gave many precepts in agriculture, beginning that type of georgic poetry later fully developed by [Luigi Alamanni](/wiki/Luigi_Alamanni "Luigi Alamanni") in his *Coltivazione*, by [Girolamo Baruffaldi](/wiki/Girolamo_Baruffaldi "Girolamo Baruffaldi") in the *Canapajo*, by [Rucellai](/wiki/Giovanni_di_Bernardo_Rucellai "Giovanni di Bernardo Rucellai") in *Le Api*, by [Bartolomeo Lorenzi](/w/index.php?title=Bartolomeo_Lorenzi&action=edit&redlink=1 "Bartolomeo Lorenzi (page does not exist)") in the *Coltivazione de' monti*, and by [Giambattista Spolverini](/wiki/Giambattista_Spolverini "Giambattista Spolverini") in the *Coltivazione del riso*.[[71]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBartoliOelsner1911903-71)

## 18th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Western_literature&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: 18th century")]

### Enlightenment era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Western_literature&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Enlightenment era")]

The [Enlightenment era](/wiki/Age_of_Enlightenment "Age of Enlightenment") was a time of progression which spanned over the 18th century across many western countries.[[72]](#cite_note-:21-72) Upon recent years, this time of "enlightenment" was split into two degrees of progression, both a "moderate" and "radical" form, and was observed to be less harmonious across regions in its nature than previously thought.[[72]](#cite_note-:21-72)

Literature has been produced to comment on the different versions of "Enlightenment" that spawned across Europe during the 18th century. Henry Farnham stated in his book *The Enlightenment in America* that the "Moderate Enlightenment [...] preaches balance, order and religious compromise", whereas the "Revolutionary Enlightenment" attempted to "construct a new heaven and earth out of the destruction of the old".[[73]](#cite_note-73)

#### Netherlands

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Western_literature&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Netherlands")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/92/Spinoza_Tractatus_Theologico-Politicus.jpg/250px-Spinoza_Tractatus_Theologico-Politicus.jpg)](/wiki/File:Spinoza_Tractatus_Theologico-Politicus.jpg)

*Tractatus Theologico-Politicus* by Spinoza

Significant texts which shaped this literary period include *[Tractatus Theologico-Politicus](/wiki/Tractatus_Theologico-Politicus "Tractatus Theologico-Politicus")*, an anonymously published treatise in Amsterdam in which the author, [Spinoza](/wiki/Baruch_Spinoza "Baruch Spinoza"), rejected the Jewish and Christian religions for their lack of depth in teaching. Spinoza discussed higher levels of philosophy in his treatise, which he suggested was only understood by elitists.[[58]](#cite_note-:4-58) This text is one of many during this period which attributed to the increasing "anti-religious" support during the time of Enlightenment.[[58]](#cite_note-:4-58) Although the book held great influence, other writers of the time rejected Spinoza's views, including theologian Lambert van Valthuysen.[[58]](#cite_note-:4-58)

#### Italy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Western_literature&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Italy")]

In the 18th century, the political condition of Italy began to improve, under [Joseph II, Holy Roman Emperor](/wiki/Joseph_II,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Joseph II, Holy Roman Emperor"), and his successors. These princes were influenced by philosophers, who in their turn felt the influence of a general movement of ideas at large in many parts of Europe, sometimes called [The Enlightenment](/wiki/The_Enlightenment "The Enlightenment").[[74]](#cite_note-74)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/83/Cesare_Beccaria.jpg/250px-Cesare_Beccaria.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cesare_Beccaria.jpg)

Portrait of [Cesare Beccaria](/wiki/Cesare_Beccaria "Cesare Beccaria") by [Eliseo Sala](/wiki/Eliseo_Sala "Eliseo Sala")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9d/Alessandro_Longhi_-_Ritratto_di_Carlo_Goldoni_%28c_1757%29_Ca_Goldoni_Venezia.jpg/250px-Alessandro_Longhi_-_Ritratto_di_Carlo_Goldoni_%28c_1757%29_Ca_Goldoni_Venezia.jpg)](/wiki/File:Alessandro_Longhi_-_Ritratto_di_Carlo_Goldoni_(c_1757)_Ca_Goldoni_Venezia.jpg)

Portrait of [Carlo Goldoni](/wiki/Carlo_Goldoni "Carlo Goldoni") by [Alessandro Longhi](/wiki/Alessandro_Longhi "Alessandro Longhi")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3e/Giuseppe_Parini_pastel_on_paper.jpg/250px-Giuseppe_Parini_pastel_on_paper.jpg)](/wiki/File:Giuseppe_Parini_pastel_on_paper.jpg)

Portrait of [Giuseppe Parini](/wiki/Giuseppe_Parini "Giuseppe Parini") by [Giuseppe Mazzola](/wiki/Giuseppe_Mazzola "Giuseppe Mazzola")

[Giambattista Vico](/wiki/Giambattista_Vico "Giambattista Vico") showed the awakening of historical consciousness in Italy. In his *Scienza nuova*, he investigated the laws governing the progress of the human race, and according to which events develop. From the psychological study of man, he tried to infer the *comune natura delle nazioni*, i.e., the universal laws of history.[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBartoliOelsner1911909-75)[[76]](#cite_note-76)

[Lodovico Antonio Muratori](/wiki/Lodovico_Antonio_Muratori "Lodovico Antonio Muratori"), after having collected in his *Rerum Italicarum scriptores* the [chronicles](/wiki/Chronicle "Chronicle"), biographies, letters and diaries of Italian history from 500 to 1500, and having discussed the most obscure historical questions in the *Antiquitates Italicae medii aevi*, wrote the *Annali d'Italia*, minutely narrating facts derived from authentic sources.[[77]](#cite_note-77) Muratori's associates in his historical research were [Scipione Maffei](/wiki/Scipione_Maffei "Scipione Maffei") of Verona and [Apostolo Zeno](/wiki/Apostolo_Zeno "Apostolo Zeno") of Venice. In his *Verona illustrata* Maffei left a treasure of learning that was also an excellent historical monograph. Zeno added much to the erudition of literary history, both in his *Dissertazioni Vossiane* and in his notes to the *Biblioteca dell'eloquenza italiana* of Monsignore [Giusto Fontanini](/wiki/Giusto_Fontanini "Giusto Fontanini"). [Girolamo Tiraboschi](/wiki/Girolamo_Tiraboschi "Girolamo Tiraboschi") and Count [Giammaria Mazzucchelli](/wiki/Giammaria_Mazzucchelli "Giammaria Mazzucchelli") of Brescia devoted themselves to literary history.[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBartoliOelsner1911909-75)

While the new spirit of the times led to the investigation of historical sources, it also encouraged inquiry into the mechanism of economic and social laws. [Ferdinando Galiani](/wiki/Ferdinando_Galiani "Ferdinando Galiani") wrote on currency; [Gaetano Filangieri](/wiki/Gaetano_Filangieri "Gaetano Filangieri") wrote a *Scienza della legislazione*. [Cesare Beccaria](/wiki/Cesare_Beccaria "Cesare Beccaria"), in his *[Trattato dei delitti e delle pene](/wiki/On_Crimes_and_Punishments "On Crimes and Punishments")*, made a contribution to the reform of the penal system and promoted the abolition of [torture](/wiki/Torture "Torture").[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBartoliOelsner1911909-75)[[78]](#cite_note-78)

The reforming movement sought to throw off the conventional and the artificial, and to return to truth. [Apostolo Zeno](/wiki/Apostolo_Zeno "Apostolo Zeno") and [Pietro Metastasio](/wiki/Pietro_Metastasio "Pietro Metastasio") had endeavoured to make [melodrama](/wiki/Melodrama "Melodrama") and reason compatible. Metastasio gave fresh expression to the affections, a natural turn to the dialogue and some interest to the plot; if he had not fallen into constant unnatural overrefinement and mawkishness, and into frequent [anachronisms](/wiki/Anachronism "Anachronism"), he might have been considered the most important writer of *[opera seria](/wiki/Opera_seria "Opera seria")* libretti and the first dramatic reformer of the 18th century.[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBartoliOelsner1911909-75)[[79]](#cite_note-79)

[Carlo Goldoni](/wiki/Carlo_Goldoni "Carlo Goldoni") overcame resistance from the old popular form of comedy, with the masks of *[pantalone](/wiki/Pantalone "Pantalone")*, of the doctor, *[harlequin](/wiki/Harlequin "Harlequin")*, [Brighella](/wiki/Brighella "Brighella"), etc., and created the comedy of character, following [Molière](/wiki/Moli%C3%A8re "Molière")'s example. Many of his comedies were written in [Venetian](/wiki/Venetian_language "Venetian language").[[80]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBartoliOelsner1911909–910-80)[[81]](#cite_note-81) His works include some of Italy's most famous and best-loved plays. Goldoni also wrote under the [pen name](/wiki/Pen_name "Pen name") and title *Polisseno Fegeio, Pastor Arcade*, which he claimed in his memoirs the "[Arcadians](/wiki/Accademia_degli_Arcadi "Accademia degli Arcadi") of Rome" bestowed on him.[[82]](#cite_note-82) One of his best-known works is the comic play *[Servant of Two Masters](/wiki/Servant_of_Two_Masters "Servant of Two Masters")*, which has been translated and adapted internationally numerous times.

The leading figure of the literary revival of the 18th century was [Giuseppe Parini](/wiki/Giuseppe_Parini "Giuseppe Parini").[[83]](#cite_note-83) In a collection of poems he published at twenty-three years of age, under the name of Ripano Eupilino, the poet shows his faculty of taking his scenes from real life, and in his satirical pieces he exhibits a spirit of outspoken opposition to his own times. Improving on the poems of his youth, he showed himself an innovator in his lyrics, rejecting at once Petrarchism, *Secentismo* and Arcadia. In the *Odi* the satirical note is already heard, but it comes out more strongly in *Del giorno*, which assumes major social and historical value. As an artist, going straight back to classical forms, he opened the way to the school of [Vittorio Alfieri](/wiki/Vittorio_Alfieri "Vittorio Alfieri"), [Ugo Foscolo](/wiki/Ugo_Foscolo "Ugo Foscolo") and [Vincenzo Monti](/wiki/Vincenzo_Monti "Vincenzo Monti"). As a work of art, the *Giorno* is sometimes a little hard and broken, as a protest against the Arcadian monotony.[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBartoliOelsner1911909-75)[[84]](#cite_note-84)

The ideas behind the [French Revolution](/wiki/French_Revolution "French Revolution") of 1789 gave a special direction to Italian literature in the second half of the 18th century. Love of liberty and desire for equality created a literature aimed at national objects, seeking to improve the condition of the country by freeing it from the double yoke of political and religious despotism.[[85]](#cite_note-85) The Italians who aspired to political redemption believed it inseparable from an intellectual revival, and thought that this could only be effected by a reunion with ancient classicism. This was a repetition of what had occurred in the first half of the 15th century.[[86]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBartoliOelsner1911910-86)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dd/VAlfieriFabre.jpg/250px-VAlfieriFabre.jpg)](/wiki/File:VAlfieriFabre.jpg)

Portrait of [Vittorio Alfieri](/wiki/Vittorio_Alfieri "Vittorio Alfieri") by [François-Xavier Fabre](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7ois-Xavier_Fabre "François-Xavier Fabre")

Patriotism and classicism were the two principles that inspired the literature that began with [Vittorio Alfieri](/wiki/Vittorio_Alfieri "Vittorio Alfieri"). He worshipped the Greek and Roman idea of popular liberty in arms against tyranny. He took the subjects of his tragedies from the history of these nations and made his ancient characters talk like revolutionists of his time. The Arcadian school, with its verbosity and triviality, was rejected.[[87]](#cite_note-87) His aim was to be brief, concise, strong and bitter, to aim at the sublime as opposed to the lowly and pastoral. He saved literature from Arcadian vacuities, leading it towards a national end, and armed himself with patriotism and classicism.[[86]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBartoliOelsner1911910-86) It is to his dramas that Alfieri is chiefly indebted for the high reputation he has attained. The appearance of the tragedies of Alfieri was perhaps the most important literary event that occurred in Italy during the 18th century.[[88]](#cite_note-88)

[Vincenzo Monti](/wiki/Vincenzo_Monti "Vincenzo Monti") was a patriot too, and wrote the *Pellegrino apostolico*, the *Bassvilliana* and the *Feroniade*; Napoleon's victories caused him to write the *Prometeo* and the *Musagonia*; in his *Fanatismo* and his *Superstizione* he attacked the [papacy](/wiki/Papacy "Papacy"); afterwards he sang the praises of the [Austrians](/wiki/Austria "Austria").[[89]](#cite_note-89) Knowing little Greek, he succeeded in translating the *Iliad* in a way remarkable for its Homeric feeling, and in his *Bassvilliana* he is on a level with Dante. In him classical poetry seemed to revive in all its florid grandeur.[[86]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBartoliOelsner1911910-86)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/57/Ugo_Foscolo.jpg/250px-Ugo_Foscolo.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ugo_Foscolo.jpg)

Portrait of [Ugo Foscolo](/wiki/Ugo_Foscolo "Ugo Foscolo") by [François-Xavier Fabre](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7ois-Xavier_Fabre "François-Xavier Fabre")

[Ugo Foscolo](/wiki/Ugo_Foscolo "Ugo Foscolo") was an eager patriot, inspired by classical models. The *Lettere di Jacopo Ortis*, inspired by [Goethe](/wiki/Johann_Wolfgang_von_Goethe "Johann Wolfgang von Goethe")'s *[The Sorrows of Young Werther](/wiki/The_Sorrows_of_Young_Werther "The Sorrows of Young Werther")*, are a love story with a mixture of patriotism; they contain a violent protest against the [Treaty of Campo Formio](/wiki/Treaty_of_Campo_Formio "Treaty of Campo Formio"), and an outburst from Foscolo's own heart about an unhappy love-affair of his. His passions were sudden and violent.[[90]](#cite_note-90) To one of these passions *Ortis* owed its origin, and it is perhaps the best and most sincere of all his writings. The *Sepolcri*, which is his best poem, was prompted by high feeling, and the mastery of versification shows wonderful art. Among his prose works a high place belongs to his translation of the *[Sentimental Journey](/wiki/A_Sentimental_Journey_Through_France_and_Italy "A Sentimental Journey Through France and Italy")* of [Laurence Sterne](/wiki/Laurence_Sterne "Laurence Sterne"), a writer by whom Foscolo was deeply affected.[[91]](#cite_note-91) He wrote for English readers some *Essays on Petrarch* and on the texts of the *Decamerone* and of Dante, which are remarkable for when they were written, and which may have initiated a new type of literary criticism in Italy. The men who made the [revolution of 1848](/wiki/Revolution_of_1848 "Revolution of 1848") were brought up in his work.[[86]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBartoliOelsner1911910-86)

#### France

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Western_literature&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: France")]

The time of enlightenment and advancement meant that both sacred and secular authors were pushing women to be at a higher level of literary knowledgeability.[[92]](#cite_note-:5-92) France was attempting to improve the education of young women and therefore have this be seen as a reflection of the advancement of society. This led to the emergence of a new genre of literature in 18th-century France of books of conduct for girls and unmarried women.[[92]](#cite_note-:5-92) Pieces by authors including Marie-Antoinette Lenoir, [Louise d'Épinay](/wiki/Louise_d%27%C3%89pinay "Louise d'Épinay") and [Anne-Thérèse de Lambert](/wiki/Anne-Th%C3%A9r%C3%A8se_de_Marguenat_de_Courcelles "Anne-Thérèse de Marguenat de Courcelles") all shared the same role of shaping young French women to lead successful and progressive lives.[[92]](#cite_note-:5-92) However, this form of education for women during the 18th century has been observed to be more oppressive than empowering.

#### Spain

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Western_literature&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Spain")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fc/Europe%2C_1700%E2%80%941714.png/500px-Europe%2C_1700%E2%80%941714.png)](/wiki/File:Europe,_1700%E2%80%941714.png)

Europe from 1700 to 1714, during the time of the Spanish Succession and the Enlightenment era

The [War of Spanish Succession](/wiki/War_of_the_Spanish_Succession "War of the Spanish Succession") (1701–1714) led to the French control over Spain. This influenced their cultural identity and, therefore, the Enlightenment period held an impact on Spanish literature in the 18th century.[[93]](#cite_note-:6-93) The court of Madrid during the 18th century saw an increase in influence from the French and the Italian,[[93]](#cite_note-:6-93) with literary influences derived increasingly from authors during the English Enlightenment period.[[94]](#cite_note-:7-94) English authors who are stated to hold influence on Spanish "Ilustrados" include [John Locke](/wiki/John_Locke "John Locke"), [Edmund Burke](/wiki/Edmund_Burke "Edmund Burke"), [Edward Young](/wiki/Edward_Young "Edward Young") and [Thomas Hobbes](/wiki/Thomas_Hobbes "Thomas Hobbes").[[94]](#cite_note-:7-94) New takes on literature began to emerge during this time, led by poets including [Ignacio de Luzán Claramunt](/wiki/Ignacio_de_Luz%C3%A1n "Ignacio de Luzán") and [Gaspar Melchor de Jovellanos](/wiki/Gaspar_Melchor_de_Jovellanos "Gaspar Melchor de Jovellanos"), who contributed greatly to the [neoclassical](/wiki/Neoclassicism "Neoclassicism") movement of the 18th century through drama and poetic forms of literature.[[95]](#cite_note-95) Only until the 20th century, however, was the Spanish Enlightenment period properly acknowledged by scholars, with past research regarding the [Spanish Enlightenment](/wiki/Enlightenment_in_Spain "Enlightenment in Spain") period as a "time of foreign imitation".[[94]](#cite_note-:7-94)

The Spanish Enlightenment held impact on women in Spain, with more women publishing literature, becoming members as well as subscribers to publications including the *Semanario de Salamanca* and the *[Diario de Madrid](/w/index.php?title=Diario_de_Madrid&action=edit&redlink=1 "Diario de Madrid (page does not exist)") [[es](https://es.wikipedia.org/wiki/Diario_de_Madrid "es:Diario de Madrid")]*.[[94]](#cite_note-:7-94) Numerous women who contributed to the Spanish Enlightenment period include poet [Margarita Hickey](/wiki/Margarita_Hickey "Margarita Hickey"), author [Frasquita Larrea](/wiki/Frasquita_Larrea "Frasquita Larrea"), and poet [María Gertrudis Hore](/wiki/Mar%C3%ADa_Gertrudis_Hore "María Gertrudis Hore").[[94]](#cite_note-:7-94)

#### Russia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Western_literature&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Russia")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/36/Feofan_Prokopovich.jpg/250px-Feofan_Prokopovich.jpg)](/wiki/File:Feofan_Prokopovich.jpg)

Portrait of Feofan Prokopovich

During the 18th century, Russia was experiencing expansions in military and geographical control, a key facet of the Enlightenment period. This is reflected in the literature of the time period.[[96]](#cite_note-:22-96) Satire and the panegyric had influenced the development of Russian literature as seen in the Russian literary figures of the time including [Feofan Prokopovich](/wiki/Feofan_Prokopovich "Feofan Prokopovich"), Kantemir, Derzhavin and [Karamzin](/wiki/Nikolay_Karamzin "Nikolay Karamzin").[[96]](#cite_note-:22-96)

### Sublime era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Western_literature&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Sublime era")]

#### Spain

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Western_literature&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Spain")]

Spanish literature of the 18th century, apart from being influenced by the Enlightenment period, was influenced by the literary concept of the "[sublime](/wiki/Sublime_(philosophy) "Sublime (philosophy)")".[[97]](#cite_note-:8-97) The "sublime" was the linkage between Spanish Neoclassical poetry and Romantic poetry prevalent during the 18th century, and was a concept of literary, [rhetorical](/wiki/Rhetoric "Rhetoric") and [philosophical](/wiki/Philosophy "Philosophy") value.[[97]](#cite_note-:8-97) Longinus described the literary devices that the sublime creates as those that allowed the reader to experience something similar to the speaker. He had created a style of language that was not used to persuade, but merely to transport the reader into the mind of the speaker.[[97]](#cite_note-:8-97)

## 19th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Western_literature&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: 19th century")]

### Romantic era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Western_literature&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Romantic era")]

#### Italy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Western_literature&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Italy")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/58/Francesco_Hayez_-_Ritratto_di_Alessandro_Manzoni.jpg/250px-Francesco_Hayez_-_Ritratto_di_Alessandro_Manzoni.jpg)](/wiki/File:Francesco_Hayez_-_Ritratto_di_Alessandro_Manzoni.jpg)

*[Portrait of Alessandro Manzoni](/wiki/Portrait_of_Alessandro_Manzoni "Portrait of Alessandro Manzoni")* by [Francesco Hayez](/wiki/Francesco_Hayez "Francesco Hayez")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b5/Giacomo_Leopardi.jpg/250px-Giacomo_Leopardi.jpg)](/wiki/File:Giacomo_Leopardi.jpg)

Portrait of [Giacomo Leopardi](/wiki/Giacomo_Leopardi "Giacomo Leopardi") by [Domenico Morelli](/wiki/Domenico_Morelli "Domenico Morelli")

The [Romantic era](/wiki/Romanticism "Romanticism") for literature was at its pinnacle during the 19th century and was a period which influenced western literature. The romantic school had as its organ the *Conciliatore* established in 1818 at Milan, on the staff of which were [Silvio Pellico](/wiki/Silvio_Pellico "Silvio Pellico"), [Ludovico di Breme](/wiki/Ludovico_di_Breme "Ludovico di Breme"), [Giovile Scalvini](/w/index.php?title=Giovile_Scalvini&action=edit&redlink=1 "Giovile Scalvini (page does not exist)"), [Tommaso Grossi](/wiki/Tommaso_Grossi "Tommaso Grossi"), [Giovanni Berchet](/wiki/Giovanni_Berchet "Giovanni Berchet"), [Samuele Biava](/w/index.php?title=Samuele_Biava&action=edit&redlink=1 "Samuele Biava (page does not exist)"), and [Alessandro Manzoni](/wiki/Alessandro_Manzoni "Alessandro Manzoni"). All were influenced by the ideas that, especially in Germany, constituted the movement called [Romanticism](/wiki/Romanticism "Romanticism").[[98]](#cite_note-98) In Italy the course of literary reform took another direction.[[99]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBartoliOelsner1911911-99) Italian writers of the 19th century, including the likes of [Leopardi](/wiki/Giacomo_Leopardi "Giacomo Leopardi") and [Alessandro Manzoni](/wiki/Alessandro_Manzoni "Alessandro Manzoni"), detested being grouped into a "category" of writing.[[100]](#cite_note-:9-100) Therefore, Italy was home to many isolated literary figures, with no unambiguous meaning for the term "Romanticism" itself. This was explained in the writings of Pietro Borsieri, in which he depicted the term Romanticism as being a literary movement that was self-defined by the writers.[[100]](#cite_note-:9-100) Contrastingly, it was noted by writers of the time, including [Giuseppe Acerbi](/wiki/Giuseppe_Acerbi "Giuseppe Acerbi"), how Italian Romantics were merely mimicking the trends seen in foreign nations in a hasty way which lacked the depth of foreign writers. Authors including [Ludovico di Breme](/wiki/Ludovico_di_Breme "Ludovico di Breme"), [Ermes Visconti](/w/index.php?title=Ermes_Visconti&action=edit&redlink=1 "Ermes Visconti (page does not exist)") [[it](https://it.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ermes_Visconti "it:Ermes Visconti")] and [Giovanni Berchet](/wiki/Giovanni_Berchet "Giovanni Berchet") did classify themselves as Romantics, however they were critiqued by others, including Gina Martegiani, who wrote in her essay "Il Romanticismo Italiano Non Esiste" of 1908 that the authors who considered themselves Romantics only created two-dimensional imitations of the works of [German Romantic](/wiki/German_Romanticism "German Romanticism") authors.[[100]](#cite_note-:9-100)

The poetry of the Romantic era of Italy was focused greatly on the motif of nature.[[5]](#cite_note-:10-5) Romantic poets drew inspiration from [ancient Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek_literature "Ancient Greek literature") and [Latin poetry](/wiki/Latin_poetry "Latin poetry") and [mythology](/wiki/Mythology "Mythology"), while poets of this time period also sought to create a sense of unity within the country with their writings.[[5]](#cite_note-:10-5) Political disunity was prevalent in 19th-century Italy, reflected in the [Risorgimento](/wiki/Unification_of_Italy "Unification of Italy").
