After the [Neapolitan Revolution](/wiki/Parthenopean_Republic "Parthenopean Republic") of 1799, the term "Risorgimento" was used in the context of a movement of "national redemption" as stated by [Antonio Gramsci](/wiki/Antonio_Gramsci "Antonio Gramsci").[[101]](#cite_note-Consolo_149–163-101) The one facet which held Italy together during this time of political disunity was the poetry and writings of the time period, as suggested by Berchet.[[5]](#cite_note-:10-5) The desire for freedom and the sense of "national redemption" is reflected heavily in the works of Italian Romantics, including [Ugo Foscolo](/wiki/Ugo_Foscolo "Ugo Foscolo"), who wrote the story *[The Last Letters of Jacopo Ortis](/wiki/The_Last_Letters_of_Jacopo_Ortis "The Last Letters of Jacopo Ortis")*, in which a man was forced to commit suicide due to the political persecutions of his country.[[102]](#cite_note-102)

The great poet of the age was [Giacomo Leopardi](/wiki/Giacomo_Leopardi "Giacomo Leopardi"). He was also an admirable prose writer. In his *Operette Morali*—dialogues and discourses marked by a cold and bitter smile at human destinies that freezes the reader—the clearness of style, the simplicity of language and the depth of conception are such that perhaps he is not only the greatest lyrical poet since Dante, but also one of the most perfect writers of prose that Italian literature has had.[[103]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBartoliOelsner1911912-103) He is widely seen as one of the most radical and challenging thinkers of the 19th century[[104]](#cite_note-104)[[105]](#cite_note-105) but routinely compared by Italian critics to his older contemporary [Alessandro Manzoni](/wiki/Alessandro_Manzoni "Alessandro Manzoni") despite expressing "diametrically opposite positions".[[106]](#cite_note-106) The strongly lyrical quality of his poetry made him a central figure on the European and international literary and cultural landscape.[[107]](#cite_note-107)

#### Britain

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Western_literature&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Britain")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/70/Lyrical_Ballads.jpg/250px-Lyrical_Ballads.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lyrical_Ballads.jpg)

*[Lyrical Ballads, with a few other poems](/wiki/Lyrical_Ballads "Lyrical Ballads")*, by William Wordsworth and Samuel Taylor Coleridge

Historical events including the [European Revolution](/wiki/Age_of_Revolution "Age of Revolution"), within which the [French revolution](/wiki/French_Revolution "French Revolution") is claimed to be most significant, contributed to the development of 19th-century [British Romanticism](/wiki/Romantic_literature_in_English "Romantic literature in English").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] These revolutions birthed a new genre of authors and poets who used their literature to convey their distaste for authority.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] This is seen in the works of poet and artist [William Blake](/wiki/William_Blake "William Blake"), who used primarily philosophical and biblical themes in his poetry, and [Samuel Taylor Coleridge](/wiki/Samuel_Taylor_Coleridge "Samuel Taylor Coleridge") and [William Wordsworth](/wiki/William_Wordsworth "William Wordsworth"), also known as the "[Lake Poets](/wiki/Lake_Poets "Lake Poets")", whose literature including the *[Lyrical Ballads](/wiki/Lyrical_Ballads "Lyrical Ballads")* is claimed to have "marked the beginning of the Romantic Movement".[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

There was known to be two waves of British Romantic authors; Coleridge and Wordsworth were grouped into the first wave, while a more radical and "aggressive" second wave of authors included the likes of [George Gordon Byron](/wiki/Lord_Byron "Lord Byron") and [Percy Bysshe Shelley](/wiki/Percy_Bysshe_Shelley "Percy Bysshe Shelley").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Due to the adamant aggression of Byron in his poetic works which advocated for an anti-violence revolution and world in which equality existed, a form of fictional character was born named the "[Byronic hero](/wiki/Byronic_hero "Byronic hero")", who is known to be rebellious in character. The Byronic hero "pervades much of his work" and Byron is considered a reflection of the character he created.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Greek and [Roman mythology](/wiki/Roman_mythology "Roman mythology") was prevalent in the works of British Romantic poets including Byron, [Keats](/wiki/John_Keats "John Keats") and [Shelley](/wiki/Percy_Bysshe_Shelley "Percy Bysshe Shelley").[[108]](#cite_note-108)[[109]](#cite_note-109)[[110]](#cite_note-110) However, there were poets who rejected the notion of mythological inspiration, including Coleridge, who preferred to take inspiration from the [Bible](/wiki/Bible "Bible") to produce significantly religious-inspired works.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

British 19th-century Romanticism developed literature which focused on the "self-organisation of living beings, their growth and adaption into their environments and the creative spark that inspired the physical system to perform complex functions".[[111]](#cite_note-:11-111) There are observed close ties between [medicine](/wiki/Medicine "Medicine"), a concept which was experiencing innovation during the 19th century, and Romantic English literature.[[111]](#cite_note-:11-111) British Romanticism also had influences from 13th-/16th-century [Italian art](/wiki/Italian_art "Italian art") as a consequence of British artists who resided in Italy during the time of Bonaparte's invasion dealing paintings to London clients from [medieval](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages") to the [High Renaissance](/wiki/High_Renaissance "High Renaissance") Italian periods.[[112]](#cite_note-:12-112) The exposure to these artworks influenced [British literature](/wiki/British_literature "British literature") and culture during a time "when Britain was struggling to prove the value of its own visual culture".[[112]](#cite_note-:12-112) The art gave inspiration and "shaped the aesthetic" of Romantic literature for writers including the likes of author [Mary Shelley](/wiki/Mary_Shelley "Mary Shelley"). The diversity and lack of standard seen in the work of infamous Italian artists including [Michelangelo](/wiki/Michelangelo "Michelangelo") and [Raphael](/wiki/Raphael "Raphael") allowed Romantic writers to celebrate new forms and ways of expression. English essayist [William Hazlitt](/wiki/William_Hazlitt "William Hazlitt") articulated how the lack of restriction, and ample artistic liberty and freedom seen through the artworks of Raphael inspired poets of the Romantic era.[[112]](#cite_note-:12-112) Michelangelo's artworks, which "embodied the sublime",[[112]](#cite_note-:12-112) were reflected in the literature of [Dante](/wiki/Dante_Alighieri "Dante Alighieri") and [Shakespeare](/wiki/William_Shakespeare "William Shakespeare"), with constant analogies being made at the time comparing the two.[[112]](#cite_note-:12-112)

## Between the 19th and 20th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Western_literature&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Between the 19th and 20th century")]

### Italy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Western_literature&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Italy")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2f/Giosu%C3%A8_Carducci2.jpg/250px-Giosu%C3%A8_Carducci2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Giosu%C3%A8_Carducci2.jpg)

[Giosuè Carducci](/wiki/Giosu%C3%A8_Carducci "Giosuè Carducci")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e5/Giuseppe_Gioachino_Belli.jpg/250px-Giuseppe_Gioachino_Belli.jpg)](/wiki/File:Giuseppe_Gioachino_Belli.jpg)

[Giuseppe Gioacchino Belli](/wiki/Giuseppe_Gioacchino_Belli "Giuseppe Gioacchino Belli")

After the *Risorgimento,* political literature became less important. The first part of this period is characterized by two divergent trends of literature that both opposed Romanticism. The first trend is the *[Scapigliatura](/wiki/Scapigliatura "Scapigliatura")*, that attempted to rejuvenate Italian culture through foreign influences, notably from the poetry of [Charles Baudelaire](/wiki/Charles_Baudelaire "Charles Baudelaire") and the works of American writer [Edgar Allan Poe](/wiki/Edgar_Allan_Poe "Edgar Allan Poe"). The second trend is represented by [Giosuè Carducci](/wiki/Giosu%C3%A8_Carducci "Giosuè Carducci"), a dominant figure of this period, fiery opponent of the Romantics and restorer of the ancient metres and spirit who, great as a poet, was scarcely less distinguished as a literary critic and historian.[[113]](#cite_note-113)

The influence of [Émile Zola](/wiki/%C3%89mile_Zola "Émile Zola") is evident in the *[Verismo](/wiki/Verismo_(literature) "Verismo (literature)")*. [Luigi Capuana](/wiki/Luigi_Capuana "Luigi Capuana") but most notably [Giovanni Verga](/wiki/Giovanni_Verga "Giovanni Verga") and were its main exponents and the authors of a verismo manifesto. Capuana published the novel *Giacinta*, generally regarded as the "manifesto" of Italian verismo.[[114]](#cite_note-114) Unlike French naturalism, which was based on [positivistic](/wiki/Positivism "Positivism") ideals, Verga and Capuana rejected claims of the scientific nature and social usefulness of the movement.

Instead *Decadentism* was based mainly on the [Decadent](/wiki/Decadent_movement "Decadent movement") style of some artists and authors of [France](/wiki/France "France") and [England](/wiki/England "England") about the end of the 19th century. The main authors of the Italian version were [Antonio Fogazzaro](/wiki/Antonio_Fogazzaro "Antonio Fogazzaro"), [Giovanni Pascoli](/wiki/Giovanni_Pascoli "Giovanni Pascoli"), best known for his *Myricae* and *Poemetti*, and [Gabriele D'Annunzio](/wiki/Gabriele_D%27Annunzio "Gabriele D'Annunzio"). Although differing stylistically, they championed idiosyncrasy and irrationality against scientific rationalism. Gabriele d'Annunzio produced original work in poetry, drama and fiction, of extraordinary originality.[[115]](#cite_note-115) He began with some lyrics distinguished no less by their exquisite beauty of form than by their licence, and these characteristics reappeared in a long series of poems, plays and novels.[[103]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBartoliOelsner1911912-103)

[Edmondo de Amicis](/wiki/Edmondo_de_Amicis "Edmondo de Amicis") is better known for his moral works and travels than for his fiction. Of the women novelists, [Matilde Serao](/wiki/Matilde_Serao "Matilde Serao") and [Grazia Deledda](/wiki/Grazia_Deledda "Grazia Deledda") became popular.[[103]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBartoliOelsner1911912-103) Deledda was awarded the 1926 Nobel Prize in Literature for her works.[[116]](#cite_note-116)

[Giovanni Prati](/wiki/Giovanni_Prati "Giovanni Prati") and [Aleardo Aleardi](/wiki/Aleardo_Aleardi "Aleardo Aleardi") continued romantic traditions. Other classical poets are [Giuseppe Chiarini](/w/index.php?title=Giuseppe_Chiarini_(literary_critic)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Giuseppe Chiarini (literary critic) (page does not exist)"), [Arturo Graf](/wiki/Arturo_Graf "Arturo Graf"), [Guido Mazzoni](/wiki/Guido_Mazzoni_(poet) "Guido Mazzoni (poet)") and [Giovanni Marradi](/wiki/Giovanni_Marradi "Giovanni Marradi"), of whom the two last named may perhaps be regarded as special disciples of Carducci. [Enrico Panzacchi](/w/index.php?title=Enrico_Panzacchi&action=edit&redlink=1 "Enrico Panzacchi (page does not exist)") was at heart still a romantic. [Olindo Guerrini](/wiki/Olindo_Guerrini "Olindo Guerrini") (who wrote under the pseudonym of Lorenzo Stecchetti) is the chief representative of *verismo* in poetry, and, though his early works obtained a *succès de scandale*, he is the author of many lyrics of intrinsic value.[[117]](#cite_note-117) [Alfredo Baccelli](/w/index.php?title=Alfredo_Baccelli&action=edit&redlink=1 "Alfredo Baccelli (page does not exist)") and [Mario Rapisardi](/wiki/Mario_Rapisardi "Mario Rapisardi") are epic poets of distinction. [Felice Cavallotti](/wiki/Felice_Cavallotti "Felice Cavallotti") is the author of the stirring *Marcia de Leonida*.[[103]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBartoliOelsner1911912-103)

Among dialect writers, the great Roman poet [Giuseppe Gioacchino Belli](/wiki/Giuseppe_Gioacchino_Belli "Giuseppe Gioacchino Belli") found numerous successors, such as [Renato Fucini](/wiki/Renato_Fucini "Renato Fucini") (Pisa) and [Cesare Pascarella](/wiki/Cesare_Pascarella "Cesare Pascarella") (Rome). Among the women poets, [Ada Negri](/wiki/Ada_Negri "Ada Negri"), with her socialistic *Fatalità* and *Tempeste*, achieved a great reputation; and others, such as [Annie Vivanti](/wiki/Annie_Vivanti "Annie Vivanti"), were highly esteemed in Italy.[[103]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBartoliOelsner1911912-103)[[118]](#cite_note-118)

Among the dramatists, [Pietro Cossa](/wiki/Pietro_Cossa "Pietro Cossa") in tragedy, [Ferdinando Martini](/wiki/Ferdinando_Martini "Ferdinando Martini"), and [Paolo Ferrari](/wiki/Paolo_Ferrari_(writer) "Paolo Ferrari (writer)") in comedy, represent the older schools. More modern methods were adopted by [Giuseppe Giacosa](/wiki/Giuseppe_Giacosa "Giuseppe Giacosa").[[103]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBartoliOelsner1911912-103)

In fiction, the historical romance fell into disfavour, though [Emilio De Marchi](/wiki/Emilio_De_Marchi_(writer) "Emilio De Marchi (writer)") produced some good examples. The novel of intrigue was cultivated by [Salvatore Farina](/wiki/Salvatore_Farina "Salvatore Farina").[[103]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBartoliOelsner1911912-103)

## 20th century and beyond

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Western_literature&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: 20th century and beyond")]

### Italy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Western_literature&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Italy")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/17/Luigi_Pirandello_1932.jpg/250px-Luigi_Pirandello_1932.jpg)](/wiki/File:Luigi_Pirandello_1932.jpg)

[Luigi Pirandello](/wiki/Luigi_Pirandello "Luigi Pirandello")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ab/Umberto_Eco_04.jpg/250px-Umberto_Eco_04.jpg)](/wiki/File:Umberto_Eco_04.jpg)

[Umberto Eco](/wiki/Umberto_Eco "Umberto Eco")

Important early-20th century writers include [Italo Svevo](/wiki/Italo_Svevo "Italo Svevo"), the author of *[La coscienza di Zeno](/wiki/Zeno%27s_Conscience "Zeno's Conscience")* (1923), and [Luigi Pirandello](/wiki/Luigi_Pirandello "Luigi Pirandello") (winner of the 1934 Nobel Prize in Literature), who explored the shifting nature of reality in his prose fiction and such plays as *Sei personaggi in cerca d'autore* (*[Six Characters in Search of an Author](/wiki/Six_Characters_in_Search_of_an_Author "Six Characters in Search of an Author")*, 1921).
[Federigo Tozzi](/wiki/Federigo_Tozzi "Federigo Tozzi") was a great novelist, critically appreciated only in recent years, and considered one of the forerunners of existentialism in the European novel.

[Grazia Deledda](/wiki/Grazia_Deledda "Grazia Deledda") was a [Sardinian](/wiki/Sardinia "Sardinia") writer who focused on the life, customs, and traditions of the [Sardinian people](/wiki/Sardinian_people "Sardinian people") in her works.[[119]](#cite_note-Migiel,_Marilyn_1994-119) In 1926 she won the Nobel Prize for literature, becoming Italy's first and only woman recipient.[[120]](#cite_note-120)

[Sibilla Aleramo](/wiki/Sibilla_Aleramo "Sibilla Aleramo") published her first novel, Una Donna (A Woman) in 1906. Today the novel is widely acknowledged as Italy's premier feminist novel.[[121]](#cite_note-121) Her writing mixes together autobiographical and fictional elements.

[Pitigrilli](/wiki/Pitigrilli "Pitigrilli") was the pseudonym of Dino Segre who published his most famous novel (cocaine) in 1921. Due to his portrayal of drug use and sex, the Catholic Church listed it as a "forbidden book". It has been translated into numerous languages, reprinted in new editions, and has become a classic.

[Maria Messina](/wiki/Maria_Messina "Maria Messina") was a Sicilian writer who focused heavily on Sicilian culture with a dominant theme being the isolation and oppression of young Sicilian women.[[122]](#cite_note-Lombardo,_Maria_Nina_1994-122) She achieved modest recognition during her life including receiving the Medaglia D'oro Prize for "La Mérica".[[122]](#cite_note-Lombardo,_Maria_Nina_1994-122)

[Anna Bantiis](/wiki/Anna_Banti "Anna Banti") most well known for her short story Il *Coraggio Delle Donne* (*The Courage of Women*) which was published in 1940.[[123]](#cite_note-Aricó_1990-123) Her autobiographical work, Un Grido Lacerante, was published in 1981 and won the Antonio Feltrinelli prize.[[123]](#cite_note-Aricó_1990-123) As well as being a successful author, Banti is recognized as a literary, cinematic, and art critic.[[123]](#cite_note-Aricó_1990-123)

[Elsa Morante](/wiki/Elsa_Morante "Elsa Morante") began writing at an early age. One of the central themes in Morante's works is narcissism. She also uses love as a metaphor in her works, saying that love can be passion and obsession and can lead to despair and destruction.[[123]](#cite_note-Aricó_1990-123) She won the Premio Viareggio award in 1948.[[124]](#cite_note-124)

[Alba de Céspedes](/wiki/Alba_de_C%C3%A9spedes "Alba de Céspedes") was a Cuban-Italian writer from Rome.[[125]](#cite_note-Nerenberg,_Ellen_1994-125) She was an anti-Fascist and was involved in the Italian Resistance.[[125]](#cite_note-Nerenberg,_Ellen_1994-125) Her work was greatly influenced by the history and culture that developed around World War II.[[125]](#cite_note-Nerenberg,_Ellen_1994-125) Although her books were bestsellers, Alba has been overlooked in recent studies of Italian women writers.[[125]](#cite_note-Nerenberg,_Ellen_1994-125)

Poetry was represented by the [Crepuscolari](/wiki/Crepuscolari "Crepuscolari") and the [Futurists](/wiki/Futurism "Futurism"); the foremost member of the latter group was [Filippo Tommaso Marinetti](/wiki/Filippo_Tommaso_Marinetti "Filippo Tommaso Marinetti"). Leading [Modernist](/wiki/Modernism "Modernism") poets from later in the century include [Salvatore Quasimodo](/wiki/Salvatore_Quasimodo "Salvatore Quasimodo") (winner of the 1959 Nobel Prize in Literature), [Giuseppe Ungaretti](/wiki/Giuseppe_Ungaretti "Giuseppe Ungaretti"), [Umberto Saba](/wiki/Umberto_Saba "Umberto Saba"), who won fame for his collection of poems *Il canzoniere*, and [Eugenio Montale](/wiki/Eugenio_Montale "Eugenio Montale") (winner of the 1975 [Nobel Prize in Literature](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Literature "Nobel Prize in Literature")). They were described by critics as "[hermeticists](/wiki/Hermeticism_(poetry) "Hermeticism (poetry)")".

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b9/Paolo_Monti_-_Servizio_fotografico_-_BEIC_6361580_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Paolo_Monti_-_Servizio_fotografico_-_BEIC_6361580_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Paolo_Monti_-_Servizio_fotografico_-_BEIC_6361580_(cropped).jpg)

[Alberto Moravia](/wiki/Alberto_Moravia "Alberto Moravia"), one of the leading figures of Italian Neorealism in literature

[Neorealism](/wiki/Neorealism_(art) "Neorealism (art)") was a movement that developed rapidly between the 1940s and the 1950s. Although its foundations were laid in the 1920s, it flourished only after the fall of Fascism in Italy, as this type of literature was not welcomed by Fascist authorities because of its social criticism and partially because some of the "new realist" authors could hold Anti-Fascist views. For example, [Alberto Moravia](/wiki/Alberto_Moravia "Alberto Moravia"), one of the leading writers of the movement, had trouble with finding a publisher for his novel which brought him fame, *[Gli indifferenti](/wiki/Gli_indifferenti "Gli indifferenti")* (1929), and after he published it, he was "driven into hiding"; [Carlo Bernari](/wiki/Carlo_Bernari "Carlo Bernari")'s *Tre operai* (1934, *Three Workers*) was unofficially banned personally by Mussolini who saw "communism" in the novel;[[126]](#cite_note-126) [Ignazio Silone](/wiki/Ignazio_Silone "Ignazio Silone") published *[Fontamara](/wiki/Fontamara "Fontamara")* (1933) in exile; [Elio Vittorini](/wiki/Elio_Vittorini "Elio Vittorini") was put in prison after publishing *[Conversazione in Sicilia](/wiki/Conversations_in_Sicily "Conversations in Sicily")* (1941). The movement was profoundly affected by the translations of socially conscious U.S. and English writers during the 1930s and 1940s, namely [Ernest Hemingway](/wiki/Ernest_Hemingway "Ernest Hemingway"), [William Faulkner](/wiki/William_Faulkner "William Faulkner"), [John Steinbeck](/wiki/John_Steinbeck "John Steinbeck"), [John Dos Passos](/wiki/John_Dos_Passos "John Dos Passos") and the others; the translators of their works, Vittorini and [Cesare Pavese](/wiki/Cesare_Pavese "Cesare Pavese"), would later become acclaimed novelists of the movement. After the war, the movement began rapidly developing and took the label "Neorealism"; Marxism and the experiences of the war became sources of inspiration for the postwar authors. Moravia wrote the novels *[The Conformist](/wiki/The_Conformist "The Conformist")* (1951) and *[La Ciociara](/wiki/Two_Women_(novel) "Two Women (novel)")* (1957), while *[The Moon and the Bonfires](/wiki/The_Moon_and_the_Bonfires "The Moon and the Bonfires")* (1949) became Pavese's most recognized work; [Primo Levi](/wiki/Primo_Levi "Primo Levi") documented his experiences in [Auschwitz](/wiki/Auschwitz "Auschwitz") in *[If This Is a Man](/wiki/If_This_Is_a_Man "If This Is a Man")* (1947); among the other writers were [Carlo Levi](/wiki/Carlo_Levi "Carlo Levi"), who reflected the experience of political exile in southern Italy in *[Christ Stopped at Eboli](/wiki/Christ_Stopped_at_Eboli "Christ Stopped at Eboli")* (1951); [Curzio Malaparte](/wiki/Curzio_Malaparte "Curzio Malaparte"), author of *[Kaputt](/wiki/Kaputt_(novel) "Kaputt (novel)")* (1944) and *[The Skin](/wiki/The_Skin_(novel) "The Skin (novel)")* (1949), novels dealing with the war on the [Eastern Front](/wiki/Eastern_Front_(World_War_II) "Eastern Front (World War II)") and in Naples; [Pier Paolo Pasolini](/wiki/Pier_Paolo_Pasolini "Pier Paolo Pasolini"), also a poet and a film director, who described the life of the Roman *[lumpenproletariat](/wiki/Lumpenproletariat "Lumpenproletariat")* in *[The Ragazzi](/wiki/Ragazzi_di_vita "Ragazzi di vita")* (1955);[[127]](#cite_note-127)[[128]](#cite_note-128) and [Corrado Alvaro](/wiki/Corrado_Alvaro "Corrado Alvaro").

[Dino Buzzati](/wiki/Dino_Buzzati "Dino Buzzati") wrote fantastic and allegorical fiction that critics have compared to [Kafka](/wiki/Franz_Kafka "Franz Kafka") and [Beckett](/wiki/Samuel_Beckett "Samuel Beckett"). [Italo Calvino](/wiki/Italo_Calvino "Italo Calvino") also ventured into fantasy in the trilogy *I nostri antenati* (*[Our Ancestors](/wiki/Our_Ancestors "Our Ancestors")*, 1952–1959) and [post-modernism](/wiki/Post-modernism "Post-modernism") in the novel *Se una notte d'inverno un viaggiatore...* (*[If on a Winter's Night a Traveller](/wiki/If_on_a_Winter%27s_Night_a_Traveler "If on a Winter's Night a Traveler")*, 1979).
[Carlo Emilio Gadda](/wiki/Carlo_Emilio_Gadda "Carlo Emilio Gadda") was the author of the experimental *Quer pasticciaccio brutto de via Merulana* (1957).

[Giuseppe Tomasi di Lampedusa](/wiki/Giuseppe_Tomasi_di_Lampedusa "Giuseppe Tomasi di Lampedusa") wrote only one novel, *[Il Gattopardo](/wiki/Il_Gattopardo "Il Gattopardo")* (*The Leopard*, 1958), but it is one of the most famous in Italian literature; it deals with the life of a [Sicilian](/wiki/Sicily "Sicily") nobleman in the 19th century.[[129]](#cite_note-129) [Leonardo Sciascia](/wiki/Leonardo_Sciascia "Leonardo Sciascia") came to public attention with his novel *[Il giorno della civetta](/wiki/The_Day_of_the_Owl "The Day of the Owl")* (*The Day of the Owl*, 1961), exposing the extent of [Mafia](/wiki/Sicilian_Mafia "Sicilian Mafia") corruption in modern Sicilian society. More recently, [Umberto Eco](/wiki/Umberto_Eco "Umberto Eco") became internationally successful with the Medieval detective story *Il nome della rosa* (*[The Name of the Rose](/wiki/The_Name_of_the_Rose "The Name of the Rose")*, 1980).

[Dacia Maraini](/wiki/Dacia_Maraini "Dacia Maraini") is one of the most successful contemporary Italian women writers. Her novels focus on the condition of women in Italy and in some works she speaks to the changes women can make for themselves and society.[[130]](#cite_note-130)

[Aldo Busi](/wiki/Aldo_Busi "Aldo Busi") is also one of the most important Italian contemporary writers. His extensive production of novels, essays, travel books and manuals provides a detailed account of modern society, especially the Italian one. He is also well known as a refined translator.

## Children's literature

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Western_literature&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Children's literature")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/65/Pinocchio.jpg/250px-Pinocchio.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pinocchio.jpg)

*[The Adventures of Pinocchio](/wiki/The_Adventures_of_Pinocchio "The Adventures of Pinocchio")* is one of the world's most translated books[[131]](#cite_note-Gasparini-131) and a canonical piece of children's literature.[[132]](#cite_note-132)

Italy has a long history of [children's literature](/wiki/Children%27s_literature "Children's literature"). In 1634, the *[Pentamerone](/wiki/Pentamerone "Pentamerone")* from Italy became the first major published collection of European folk tales.[[133]](#cite_note-reynolds-133): 7 The *Pentamerone* contained the first literary European version of the story of [Cinderella](/wiki/Cinderella "Cinderella"). The author, [Giambattista Basile](/wiki/Giambattista_Basile "Giambattista Basile"), created collections of fairy tales that include the oldest recorded forms of many well-known European fairy tales.[[134]](#cite_note-134) In the 1550s, [Giovanni Francesco Straparola](/wiki/Giovanni_Francesco_Straparola "Giovanni Francesco Straparola") released *[The Facetious Nights of Straparola](/wiki/The_Facetious_Nights_of_Straparola "The Facetious Nights of Straparola")*. Called the first European storybook to contain fairy tales, it eventually had 75 separate stories, albeit intended for an adult audience.[[135]](#cite_note-135) [Giulio Cesare Croce](/wiki/Giulio_Cesare_Croce "Giulio Cesare Croce") also borrowed from stories children enjoyed for his books.[[136]](#cite_note-Silvey-136): 757

In 1883, Carlo Collodi wrote *[The Adventures of Pinocchio](/wiki/The_Adventures_of_Pinocchio "The Adventures of Pinocchio")*, the first Italian fantasy novel. In the same year, [Emilio Salgari](/wiki/Emilio_Salgari "Emilio Salgari"), the man who would become "the adventure writer par excellence for the young in Italy"[[137]](#cite_note-137) published for the first time his *[Sandokan](/wiki/Sandokan "Sandokan")*. In the 20th century, Italian children's literature was represented by such writers as [Gianni Rodari](/wiki/Gianni_Rodari "Gianni Rodari"), author of *[Il romanzo di Cipollino](/wiki/Il_romanzo_di_Cipollino "Il romanzo di Cipollino")*, and [Nicoletta Costa](/wiki/Nicoletta_Costa "Nicoletta Costa"), creator of Julian Rabbit and Olga the Cloud.[[138]](#cite_note-Oxford-138)[[139]](#cite_note-repubblica-139)
